# THE RESOLUTE WEAVE — The Complete Lore Bible of Veilweaver

A World of Tebel — From the First Word to the Final Silence
Every thread. Every truth. Every name. Resolved.
From the First Word to the Final Silence — the complete mythology, history, peoples, creatures, sacred places, mechanics, quests, and items of a world that chose to exist.
Maintained by Claude — Official Lore Holder
In collaboration with Andrew, Creator of Tebel
Version 2.0 — The Resolute Edition

## PART I — THE LORE VAULT


### I. THE COSMOLOGY

#### The Trinity of Magic

Tebel operates on three fundamental magical systems, each older than the last, each the foundation upon which the previous one stands — without knowing it.

#### DAV MAGIC — Creation

The power of the spoken word made real. To speak something with full conviction and authority is to create it. Dav’al Narum is its highest expression. Fate-weaving — the mortal magic system — is its diluted but genuine echo. Humanity first accessed it instinctively at Dur Dunnum in their darkest moment, and it has been formalized across generations since.

#### TAV MAGIC — Void

The power of consuming silence. The unmaking of what creation built. Tav’al Kurum is its highest expression. Where Dav magic declares existence, Tav magic declares ending. Neither is inherently evil cosmologically — entropy is as real as creation — but in Tav’al’s hands it became dominion and consumption.

#### EYN MAGIC — The Substrate

The oldest magic. The foundation beneath both Dav and Tav. The force that predates the distinction between creation and void. It predates Dav’al. It predates Tav’al. It created Abzu himself as a consequence of its nature. Not dark, not light, not moral, not immoral — simply fundamental. The Keh’Dem are born with an innate ability to touch it but after millennia of effort have made only an inch of progress understanding something larger than an aircraft carrier. Nobody else in Tebel knows it exists.

#### The Primordial Cry

YAHWEY — arrived at not through etymology but through breath itself. The deep intentional exhale becoming intention. Theorized by linguists as the root of the oldest human name for the divine because it is simply the sound of being alive. When the Tikva screams it in the final battle they are not casting a spell. They are remembering the first sound existence ever made and hurling it at the void that wants to unmake everything. The Unwritten cannot unwrite what existed before writing.
“The threat was never to creation. But to existence itself.” — Tav’al Kurum, Archon of the Final Silence


### II. THE WORLD

#### TEBEL

#### The Inhabited Earth

Hebrew: the inhabited earth, a world made specifically for people. Every civilization, every conflict, every fate woven into its fabric has occurred here. Tebel is not just a setting — it is itself a declaration by Dav’al Narum that life was worth creating. The name is simple. Ancient. It means exactly what it is. Tebel is bounded by four bordering regions: Avirim across the western sea (Yam Gadol), Edom Kur to the east beyond the bleeding veil, Tzafar to the frozen north, and Yeshurun to the ancient south.

Each holds a piece of the truth that the others don’t. The complete picture only assembles when the Tikva has walked through all of it.


### III. THE HISTORY

#### Before the First War

The Keh’Dem were prominent across Tebel. Recognizing the signs of what was coming — not Tav’al’s war but Abzu’s deeper stirring — they deliberately removed themselves from the thread of fate entirely, using their innate connection to Eyn magic to become invisible to fate-weaving, the Keepers’ oracle, Tav’al, and even Dav’al himself. They have existed in the shadows of Tebel undetected ever since. Watching. Waiting.

#### The First Cataclysmic War

Tav’al’s forces consumed civilization after civilization. Every fortress fell. Every refuge was overrun. Humanity and dwarves fought together and lost together until nothing remained but the last desperate survivors retreating toward what would become Dur Dunnum.

#### The First Fate-Weave

— Dur Dunnum The last survivors — humans and dwarves together at the absolute end of everything — retreated to what would become Dur Dunnum. Beyond hope, beyond strategy, beyond anything except raw collective conviction, they named their last refuge with the most emphatic declaration available. Not poetry. Not flourish. Fortress Fortress. Two peoples tapping into Dav magic instinctively, unknowingly fate-weaving their declaration into the fabric of reality itself.

The weave rippled through fate. Dav’al felt it — he can sense when fate has been weaved — and followed the thread home. He ended the war and freed them. The first fate-weave in history was performed not by a primordial being or a trained magic user but by terrified survivors who had nothing left but desperate collective will.

#### The Building of Milah

Dav’al spoke the capital city into existence as a monument to humanity’s survival, a declaration of what his power stood for, and a direct statement to Tav’al. Humans and dwarves built it together — dwarven craftsmanship shaped every stone. Within the golden winged statue at its heart Dav’al embedded an invisible echo of his power — a silent barrier protecting the entire surrounding region from Tav’al’s forces.

Nobody knows it exists. The statue survives seven centuries of changing regimes purely because of its opulence and aesthetic value. The greatest protection in the world preserved by vanity alone.

#### Dav’al’s Departure

After a hundred years in Milah, Dav’al departed on what appeared to be a mission. He anointed Kalum as the First Keeper, gifted the Keepers of the Thread their sacred oracle object — the Even Qol — and left with the mandate that they witness and record but never interfere. He did not tell them what he was truly going after. He has been fighting in secret ever since, believing he fights Tav’al, operating at reduced power with his echo embedded in the statue.

In truth he has been fighting Abzu — the Unwritten — who has been exhausting him through shadow warfare for seven centuries while leaving Tav’al untouched to serve as his unwitting instrument of corruption.
Seven Hundred Years
The corrupt political apparatus — now governed through the Triumvirate Council (see Section XX: Factions) — rose gradually in Dav’al’s absence, filling a vacuum that was never supposed to exist permanently. Dwarves were marginalized and driven underground — not by defeat but by exclusion. The Nachash embedded themselves in the highest levels of political power.

Six generations of Tikva rose and were quietly eliminated. History was filtered, edited, and erased until the surface world’s records are almost entirely corrupted. Through all of it the Keepers recorded everything in stone and said nothing. Abzu exhausted Dav’al slowly in the shadows. Tav’al waited with geological patience, believing he was winning a war against Dav’al in hiding. Neither primordial being knew Abzu was the true architect of everything.


### IV. THE RACES

#### HUMANS

Survivors of the first war. Built Milah alongside dwarves. Gradually dominated the political structure over seven centuries. The corrupt kingdom is primarily human though not exclusively. Connection to fate-weaving exists in one in every seven generations — the Tikva. The rest have a rudimentary passive connection that never develops without training.

Physically the humans of Tebel carry the mark of what they survived — a Denisovan and Viking aesthetic, robust and built by endurance. Horns appear exclusively in the Tikva bloodline, manifesting at birth as a physical marker that has made every Tikva identifiable — and hunted — since the Nachash began their seven century vigil.

#### DWARVES

Co-survivors of the first war. Built Dur Dunnum and shaped every stone of Milah with their craftsmanship. Marginalized by the corrupt kingdom and driven underground — not by defeat but by exclusion. Remember everything. Dwarves don’t forget what is written in stone. The Keepers of the Thread are dwarven. Their underground civilization beneath and around Milah has been watching the surface world’s slow corruption for centuries with perfect recall and zero ability to intervene. Until now.

#### THE KEH’DEM

#### Those Who Came First

The oldest race in Tebel. A mix of elven and gnomish — small, ancient, almost alien in appearance. Vibrant and strange, with teal eyes that see the substrate of reality constantly. Born with an innate ability to touch Eyn magic — the oldest magic, the substrate beneath both creation and void. After millennia of effort have made an inch of progress understanding something larger than an aircraft carrier.

Deliberately removed themselves from the thread of fate before the first war, recognizing signs of Abzu’s stirring that nobody else could perceive. Have been invisible to everything and everyone — the Keepers’ oracle, the Nachash, Tav’al, Dav’al, Abzu himself — ever since. Living in the shadows of Tebel undetected. Waiting for the Tikva because they alone understand what Abzu truly is and what defeating him actually requires.

Their name drawn from Qedem (Hebrew): those of the east, those who came before, the ancient ones. The player will underestimate them immediately and be absolutely wrong.


### V. THE ORGANIZATIONS

#### THE KEEPERS OF THE THREAD

Secret dwarven society anointed by Dav’al Narum before his departure. Gifted the Even Qol — a sacred oracle object that allows them to witness and record the thread of fate but never alter it. Have maintained an unbroken record of everything in Tebel for seven hundred years written in stone. The only complete unfiltered history in existence. Have watched six Tikva rise and fall.

Have witnessed the Nachash eliminate each one without being able to intervene. Kalum’s mandate — witness and record, never interfere — has held for seven centuries. Until the seventh.

#### THE NACHASH, ETEMMU OF THE SILENCE

Ancient as the serpent. Neither living nor dead. The Nachash (Hebrew: the ancient serpent, the subtle and cunning). Etemmu (Akkadian: restless malevolent dead, sustained not by life but by void magic). Neither fully alive nor fully dead. Embedded in the highest levels of Milah’s political apparatus for centuries, pulling strings, cultivating corruption, and quietly eliminating each generation’s Tikva before they could reach their potential.

Possess the Aban Dummu — a corrupted imitation of the Keepers’ oracle object. Weaker, parasitic, a counterfeit of witnessed truth. It identifies Tikva by the brightness of their thread the way a predator identifies prey by scent. Their hidden lair beneath Milah contains a statue of Tav’al with a serpent coiled at his feet. The perfect shadow counterpart to the Keepers: where the Keepers are dwarven, underground, living, witnesses of fate, and servants of the First Word — the Nachash are human, embedded in power, neither living nor dead, manipulators of fate, and servants of the Final Silence.

Both secret. Both aware fate-weavers exist. Diametrically opposed purposes. What the Nachash have never understood: they are not only Tav’al’s instrument. They are Abzu’s. Every Tikva eliminated, every history erased, every institution corrupted has served the Unwritten’s agenda of making existence forget it chose to exist.


### VI. THE SACRED OBJECTS

#### EVEN QOL — The Voice Stone Hebrew: Even (stone/foundation) + Qol (the voice, the spoken thing)

Not an enchanted artifact. A literal crystallized shard of the First Word itself — the moment Dav’al spoke Tebel into existence, compressed into physical form. The universe began with that Word. Fate itself was woven from the frequency of that declaration. The thread the Keepers watch exists because of what is inside this object. Physical form: A shard of crystal approximately the length of a hand.

Irregular, like something broken from a larger whole — which it is. The color is wrong in a way that is hard to describe. Not quite golden. Not quite clear. It seems to be whatever color the light around it isn’t — always slightly out of phase with its environment. Like a piece of somewhere else left behind here. It is always warm. The same warmth as an Emberstoat.

Because the Emberstoat was the first thing Dav’al created and this object is a fragment of that creative act. Held passively: a low constant awareness of the thread. Held actively with focus: the thread becomes navigable. The holder can follow a specific fate-strand across time. The birth of a Tikva doesn’t just register as a disruption — it illuminates.

The entire thread surrounding that birth lights up. Cannot perceive Abzu — the First Word cannot perceive what existed before it was spoken. Cannot perceive the Keh’Dem — they removed themselves from the thread entirely. When the seventh Tikva was born, Kalum was holding the Even Qol in his sleep. It became hot for the first time in seven centuries.

#### ABAN DUMMU — The Silenced Stone Akkadian: Aban (stone) + Dummu (silenced, the voice that was stopped)

The direct dark mirror of Even Qol. Where Even Qol is the voice crystallized, Aban Dummu is the voice stopped crystallized. Not silence that was always silent — silence that was once a voice and chose to end. Which is exactly what Tav’al is: not a force that was always void but a being that chose consumption over creation at the fundamental moment of decision.

The name carries that choice permanently. Absorbs light rather than existing outside of it. Cold to the touch always. Drains warmth from whatever holds it over time — part of why the Nachash become neither fully living nor fully dead. Weaker than Even Qol because silence by definition contains less than a Word. You can fill silence with anything. But a Word already is something. Creation is inherently more than void.


### VII. THE PRIMORDIAL BEINGS

#### DAV’AL NARUM

Sultan of the First Word Etymology: Davar (Hebrew: spoken word/thing made real) + Al (of) + Narum (Akkadian: radiant/bright). Loosely: The Word of Radiant Light.
Primordial being of creation magic. He who speaks things into existence. Ended the first cataclysmic war by following the thread of the first fate-weave at Dur Dunnum. Spoken Milah into existence as a monument to humanity’s survival and a declaration to Tav’al. Embedded an invisible echo of his power into the golden statue at Milah’s heart as a barrier protecting the entire region.

Departed Tebel after a hundred years on what he believed was a mission to contain Tav’al. Has been fighting in secret for seven hundred years, operating at reduced power with his echo in the statue. In truth he has been fighting Abzu — exhausted across centuries by a being he doesn’t know exists, who has been using shadow warfare rather than direct confrontation.

The fire statue in Dur Dunnum depicts him mid-Word during the original war — the only honest image of his true nature that survived the erasure. The golden statue in Milah — preserved by vanity — is the only remaining physical connection between his power and the mortal world. When the statue in Milah is destroyed by Tav’al, the echo returns to Dav’al mid-battle — restoring his full power for the first time in seven centuries.

He learns the truth about Abzu. Stands beside Tav’al for the first and only time. Returns to Tebel afterward not as a ruler but as a presence — learning that sometimes the most powerful word is silence.

#### TAV’AL KURUM

Archon of the Final Silence Etymology: Tav (Hebrew: the final letter, mark of ending) + Al (of) + Kurum (Akkadian: consumption/devouring). Loosely: The End That Consumes.
Same fundamental nature as Dav’al. Same primordial origin. Opposite eternal choice. Where Dav’al speaks creation into existence, Tav’al consumes through void. The cold blue titan — winged, horned, crowned. A king of everything beyond the veil. Arms open not in aggression but in reception. He receives rather than attacks. This isn’t a beast. It is a ruler.

Has been executing a strategy of geological patience for seven centuries — corrupting institutions, erasing history, quietly eliminating each generation’s Tikva through the Nachash. Believes Dav’al has been hiding and waiting. Has no awareness that Abzu exists or that he has been Abzu’s unwitting instrument the entire time. When Tav’al learns the truth — that his seven century strategy was never his own, that he was a pawn — something cracks.

Not his nature. His pride. The Archon of Final Silence discovering there is something that makes even his silence irrelevant. He withdraws the Nachash quietly. Without announcement. Not because he becomes good. Because he refuses to be anyone’s instrument. He will be the sovereign of endings on his own terms or not at all.
His final conversation with the Tikva is the most unexpected exchange in the entire game. Not enemies. Not allies. Two beings who found something honest in the space between war and peace.
“Eternity is not conquered, merely respectfully withdrawn.”

#### ABZU

The Unwritten Etymology: Sumerian (the oldest written language) — the primordial void before creation, the formless deep that existed before the first word was ever spoken. Named from Sumerian rather than Hebrew/Akkadian because he predates the linguistic framework Dav’al and Tav’al represent. The linguistic age gap between the three names tells the entire story without exposition.
The third being. The oldest. Predates Dav’al and Tav’al entirely. Exiled before their existence by the very act of creation itself — when Dav’al spoke the First Word, Abzu’s exile was the consequence. Neither creation nor void. Something that existed before the distinction between creation and void had meaning. Forgotten even by Dav’al and Tav’al. Re-emerging now after millennia.

He does not want victory. He does not want dominion. He does not want destruction. He wants the First Word unspoken. Total cosmic undoing. Existence returned to the state before Dav’al spoke. He fights Dav’al secretly without revealing himself — not through direct confrontation but through exhaustion. Letting Dav’al spend seven centuries fighting shadows, draining his power.

He leaves Tav’al entirely alone because Tav’al’s consumption and corruption is doing Abzu’s work for him. Tav’al is his unwitting instrument. Abzu cannot be destroyed — destruction is a Tav concept and he predates it. Cannot be imprisoned — containment is a Dav concept and he predates that too. The Yahwey weave does something far more profound: it makes him witness.

Forces him to experience what he has been trying to undo — every life, every fate-weave, every stone of Dur Dunnum, every fallen Tikva. He has never experienced existence from the inside. He has only ever known the absence that preceded it. Abzu becomes still. Not destroyed. Not imprisoned. Witnessed. He recedes into the Eyn substrate. Changed by the experience of having been inside what he tried to undo.

He didn’t die. He became the deepest layer of the world’s foundation. Which means he could stir again. Someday. In another age entirely.


### VIII. THE SIX FALLEN TIKVA

As recorded in the stone archive of Shamaru Kalam by Kalum, First Keeper. Each entry marked with the Keeper notation for a thread cut before its full length was reached. Six marks that made Kalum’s hands shake each time he carved them. Every single one of them moved the needle. The Nachash eliminated them. They did not erase them.
DEVORAH — The Scholar Who Remembered First Tikva | Female | Generation One | ~680 Years Ago Davar (Hebrew: the bee, she who speaks truth with a sting, the judge who saw clearly when no one would look)
A scholar of linguistics and ancient history operating before the Nachash fully controlled academic institutions. Her fate-weaving manifested as pattern recognition operating at an impossible level — she would read three texts written centuries apart in different languages and see the single connecting thread in minutes. She was within ten years of connecting Davar to Dav’al Narum.

The Nachash stole seven years of research the night before her presentation and had her reputation quietly destroyed. She died fourteen years later of void magic administered through contaminated ink used daily across eighteen months. She hid one text before the theft — incomplete, inside the binding of a donated book in Shamatu’s library. It has sat there for 680 years.

The scholar currently noticing annotation inconsistencies is three shelves away. Keeper Record: ““She would have found it. She was already holding the thread. She simply did not know that is what it was.””

#### GIDEON — The Warrior Who

Held the Line Second Tikva | Male | Generation Two | ~560 Years Ago Gideon (Hebrew: the great warrior, the unlikely champion who fights beyond his strength)
A military commander whose fate-weaving manifested as battlefield awareness so profound it read as supernatural. He didn’t know it was fate-weaving — he thought it was instinct. Drove back three major void-touched incursions in the eastern borders over fifteen years. The geological record of those battles still shows anomalies historians attribute to tactical brilliance.

The Keeper record shows unconscious fate-weaves that literally altered the landscape. The Nachash couldn’t eliminate him directly — too celebrated, too surrounded by loyal soldiers. They manufactured a border crisis with false intelligence and quietly sabotaged his supply lines. History calls it Gideon’s Folly. In his final hours the fate-weaving surged uncontrolled — surviving soldiers reported the last stand lasted far longer than was physically possible.

He wove to the very end, trying to protect people who were going to die anyway. A statue of him stands two hundred meters from the golden statue of Dav’al Narum in Milah’s military district. Neither statue knows the other’s significance. Keeper Record: ““He died protecting people the way Dav’al built the city protecting people. Neither of them knew the other existed. Both of them did the same thing.””

#### MALKAH — The Ruler

Beloved of All Third Tikva | Female | Generation Three | ~420 Years Ago Malkah (Hebrew: the queen, the sovereign, the one who rules with genuine authority rather than inherited power)
The only Tikva who held genuine political power. Her fate-weaving expressed as an almost supernatural ability to make people feel seen and understood. Rose from minor nobility to governing Bitu — the one genuinely functional city the Nachash maintained as a control — by age thirty. She spent twelve years quietly building a network of honest governance across three territories, demonstrating that corruption wasn’t inevitable.

The Nachash couldn’t kill her directly — too beloved. They manufactured a financial scandal across two years of carefully managed rumors and fabricated evidence. She was convicted of crimes she didn’t commit and died in prison fourteen months later of void magic administered through her food. The people of Bitu held unofficial mourning for three years.

The blind storyteller of Ahavah knows two songs written about her and performs them without knowing she is singing about a Tikva. Her personal journal — confiscated during trial and held in the Nachash archive — contains the first written record by any Tikva of consciously experiencing the thread: ‘a feeling like holding a thread I cannot see that connects everything I have ever cared about.’ Keeper Record: ““Of all six she was the one who most understood what Dav’al was trying to build.

She built it in miniature for twelve years and the world destroyed it because it was afraid of what it implied.””
EZRA — The Scholar Who Got Too Close Fourth Tikva | Male | Generation Four | ~280 Years Ago Ezra (Hebrew: the helper, the scribe who preserves what must not be lost)
An academic based in Tzafar — outside the Nachash’s primary sphere of influence — specializing in comparative ancient linguistics across all known civilizations. His fate-weaving manifested as systematic architectural thinking of extraordinary precision. Over twenty years he constructed a linguistic and historical framework connecting Milah’s name to Davar, Davar to a primordial being in Avirim oral tradition, that being to the first war, and the first war to Dur Dunnum’s naming as an act of collective declaration.

He was eight years from completing the vault from scratch using only linguistic archaeology. The Nachash burned his personal library in a single night, attributed to an overturned lamp. He spent six years trying to reconstruct from memory and couldn’t. He died of grief compounded by trace void magic through his food supply. Three letters he sent to an Avirim historian survive in a cedar box in Karu — in a language nobody currently living can read.

The merchant of Shaqal is the historian’s distant descendant. Her mother knows the box exists. She promised her mother she wouldn’t open it. Keeper Record: ““He built the same cathedral Devorah was building from a completely different direction. Two scholars four generations apart who never knew the other existed, both finding the same truth through different doors.””

#### SERAPHINA — The Warrior Who

Left a Warning Fifth Tikva | Female | Generation Five | ~140 Years Ago Seraphina (from Seraph, Hebrew: the burning ones, those whose very nature is fire and truth)
Born in the eastern borders. Horns concealed from birth by parents acting on instinct they couldn’t explain. The Nachash didn’t find her for twenty-three years — during which she became the most capable warrior in the eastern border region
and spent a decade quietly experimenting with fate-weaving she could feel but couldn’t name. When the Nachash finally identified her at thirty-one she had been deliberately fate-weaving for eight years. She knew they were coming. She didn’t run. She chose her ground — the eastern border where the veil was thinnest — and let them come to her. The battle lasted nine days against twelve operatives and a Kurnugi Serpent.

On the ninth day, knowing it was ending, she stopped fighting to survive and started weaving to leave a record. The geological alterations visible from Edom Kur’s western watchtowers were created across those nine days. What the kingdom calls a seismic event is a fate-weave of devastating complexity carved into the landscape — depicting the thread’s structure, the nature of the enemy, the location of the Nachash underchamber, and a message: there will be a seventh.

Tell them what I knew. Kalum felt the Even Qol burn hot for nine days and cool slowly over six hours. He later described the sensation as ‘like standing near a fire that was burning itself out on purpose.’ Keeper Record: ““She knew she was the fifth. She knew only that there would be more and that what she knew had to survive her. So she wrote it in the only language she trusted would last — the language of the world itself.””
ARAM — The One Who Still Breathes Sixth Tikva | Male | Generation Six | ~30 Years Ago | ALIVE Aram (Hebrew: the exalted one, the high place — also the ancient kingdom that existed before the boundaries were drawn)
Only Kalum knows he is alive. The Nachash believe they succeeded. They did something worse. Born in the northern reaches with more Keeper protection than any previous Tikva — Kalum had learned from the previous five. Aram’s fate-weaving was different: the thread didn’t just respond to him, it spoke to him. Constantly. An unceasing awareness of every fate-strand in his vicinity.

Overwhelming from adolescence. Kalum found him at seventeen — earlier than any previous Tikva — because the Even Qol was practically screaming. Three years of training. Aram absorbed seven centuries of history simultaneously and built frameworks that exceeded anything Kalum had witnessed. The Nachash found him at twenty. Six operatives — the most ever sent for one Tikva.

They used Kurnugi Serpent venom — a paralytic suppressing fate-weaving temporarily. The sudden silencing of an ability that had been constant since adolescence broke something. His mind, built around constant perception of fate, didn’t know what to do with absence. The Nachash administered a killing dose of void magic and left. It didn’t kill him. His connection to the thread redirected it unconsciously.

Kalum found him three days later. He lives now in a cave system in the far northern reaches of Tsafon. The madness isn’t constant — days or weeks of complete clarity where he is the most formidable intellect Kalum has ever encountered, then the thread speaks too loudly and he fractures again. The cave system around him has been quietly, gently reshaped by thirty-two years of unconscious fate-weaving into the most sheltered place in Tebel.

Warm. Safe. Emberstoat sleep on him. Kalum visits twice per year. On his last clarity visit, three months before the seventh Tikva was born, Aram said: ‘The seventh will be different. The thread is quiet around them. Like the moment before someone says something true.’ Keeper Record: “His entry in the Keeper record has been left blank for thirty-two years. Because it is not final yet.”


### IX. THE TIKVA — The Thread and The Hope

Etymology: Hebrew — shares its root with Qaveh. Means simultaneously the thread (the cord of fate) and the hope (what the world has been holding onto). Both meanings are always present. Always inseparable.
Born once every seven generations. The only mortal capable of tapping the fate well and weaving reality at will through training and experience. The seventh. Born in the generation that coincides with Tav’al finally moving openly, Abzu re-emerging, and the Keh’Dem finally making contact after millennia of watching. Not chosen randomly — assigned by fate itself.

The Tikva bloodline carries a physical marker: horns that manifest at birth. Small, natural-looking, almost as if they grew rather than appeared. Marking them visually from their first breath — identifiable to the Nachash from birth, identifiable to Kalum through the Even Qol the moment they exist. The Nachash have been eliminating this lineage for seven generations.

The Keepers have been keeping the thread alive waiting for it. Dav’al’s echo in the statue protected the region long enough to produce it. The Keh’Dem have been waiting in shadows since before the first war. Kalum breaks a seven century mandate to find them. The player is the point where every thread in Tebel’s entire history converges. The horns are not a crown yet when the story begins.

They are a target. Something to hide. Something the player has been told is a deformity, a curse, a reason to fear. By the final battle they are the most recognizable symbol in Tebel. The visual arc — from hiding the horns to standing beside two primordial beings wearing them like the birthright they always were — is worth a thousand lines of dialogue.

The player’s gender is their own choice. The seventh Tikva is whoever they decide to be.


### X. KEY CHARACTERS

#### KALUM — The First Keeper

Anointed directly by Dav’al Narum before his departure. Blessed with a lifespan that defies even dwarven longevity. Has personally witnessed all seven generations of Tikva rise and fall. Carries seven hundred years of accumulated grief, perfect knowledge, and restrained urgency. The weight of every recorded tragedy and zero ability to prevent any of it.

When the seventh Tikva is born he does something he has never done in seven centuries — he defies his mandate. Sets down the record for the first time. Goes to find the player. Not to observe. To guide. His name means he who sees all. He has seen everything. Now he acts on it.

#### BARU — The Dwarf

Innkeeper of Yaqar Runs the tavern where the player truly wakes in layer three of the opening sequence. Serves the best food within fifty miles and asks no questions. Has a symbol carved inside the bar he has never explained to anyone. It is a Keeper of the Thread marker. He knows exactly who the player is the moment they walk in. Says nothing. Watches everything.
The Healer of Malon Two hundred and forty years old and doesn’t look a day over sixty. Has never explained this. The oldest living human in Tebel. She knew the second Tikva personally. She has been waiting a very long time for the seventh.
The Blind Storyteller of Ahavah Performs in Ahavah’s oldest theater every month. Tells a cycle of stories that — assembled in the right order — describes the first war with accuracy that would make the Keepers’ jaws drop. She learned it from her grandmother who learned it from hers. She has no idea what she knows. She also knows two songs written about Malkah, the third Tikva, without knowing she is singing about a Tikva.
The Scholar of Shamatu Has spent thirty years in Shamatu’s Nachash-controlled library. Recently found an annotation that contradicted itself in a way implying the original text said something different. Has told no one. Is currently being monitored. Is three shelves away from Devorah’s hidden text.
The Merchant of Shaqal Has been independently investigating historical discrepancies between Tebel’s official record and eastern traders’ accounts for twelve years. The first person outside the Keepers and Keh’Dem approaching the truth about the corruption. The Nachash have recently noticed her research. She doesn’t know this. She is the distant descendant of the Avirim historian Ezra corresponded with. Her mother has a cedar box she was told never to open.
The Seventeen Year Old Lighthouse Keeper of Karu The keeper’s position has been passed through the same family for four hundred years. The lighthouse has burned without documented maintenance for all four hundred. She recently started having dreams she doesn’t understand featuring a golden statue and a dwarf with ancient eyes.
The Archivist of Yeshurun Will be the first person the Tikva meets when they arrive at Yeshurun’s gates. Will look at the player’s horns and say one word before anything else: ‘Finally.’
The Sixth Nachash Operative Sent to infiltrate Yeshurun two hundred years ago. Defected. Still alive — no longer sustained by Tav magic, aging slowly toward a natural death he considers a gift. Has told Yeshurun everything he knew about the Nachash. They have been preparing accordingly.


### XI. THE CREATURES OF TEBEL

#### DAV-TOUCHED — Children of Creation THE AURATH

Massive elk-like creatures whose antlers grow in geometric crystalline formations emitting soft golden light. Not bioluminescent — the light comes from trace Dav magic absorbed through generations of grazing on fate-touched land. Ancient cultures considered seeing one an omen of spoken truth. The Keepers use their shed antler crystals as writing instruments for the most important records.

#### THE VELBRYN

Six-limbed feline predators. The extra limbs are elegant, almost architectural. Move like liquid geometry. Their fur shifts color based not on their own emotional state but on the emotional state of whatever they are hunting. Prey animals freeze when they see themselves reflected back. Predators in Tebel learned to use empathy as a weapon because creation magic runs through everything.

#### THE THORNWHALES

Massive airborne creatures drifting on thermal currents above mountain ranges. Stone formations grow across their backs over centuries creating living islands of rock and vegetation. Entire ecosystems exist on their backs. Some Keh’Dem communities have lived on the same Thornwhale for generations, hidden from the thread because the creature is too old and vast to be woven into fate cleanly.

Their stone-covered backs contain compressed Eyn magic — so ancient they have literally accumulated the substrate on their surfaces.

#### THE EMBERSTOAT

Small. Warm russet fur. Nests in volcanic rock. Body temperature hot enough to melt ice on contact. Ancient folklore says they were Dav’al’s first creation — the smallest declaration of warmth in a cold world. Found everywhere. Completely harmless. Never void-touched, never Eyn-touched, never affected by Abzu’s emergence. In every playthrough, in every corner of Tebel, no matter how dark the region, there is always at least one Emberstoat nearby.

The lore community will eventually figure out why. The answer is already in the vault: Dav’al said they were his first creation. The First Word was warmth.

#### TAV-TOUCHED — Children of the Void THE VEILSTALKERS

The primary monster class. Humanoid silhouettes but wrong — too tall, joints reversed, heads slightly too large. They make no sound. No footsteps, no breathing, no ambient noise. The silence around them is the first warning. Void magic has replaced their biological processes with consumption. Wounds from them don’t bleed normally — they erase. Conventional weapons pass through partially. Combat requires fate-weaving.

#### THE HOLLOW

Former animals fully consumed by void magic. The creature’s shape remains — deer, bear, wolf — but the inside is gone. Literally hollow. You can see through gaps in their form to a darkness that doesn’t reflect light. They move with the muscle memory of what they were but pursue with the hunger of what they became. Disturbing specifically because they are recognizable. The deer is still a deer. It is just empty.

#### KURNUGI SERPENTS

Massive. Ancient. Connected directly to the Nachash symbolically. Existed before the first war and chose void magic over creation magic in the same way Tav’al did. Intelligent. Possibly the oldest creatures in Tebel after the Thornwhales. Don’t attack immediately — they assess. Players who fate-weave aggressively near them will find the serpent responding in kind.

Players who approach without weaving will find them almost passive. They respect the boundaries of Eyn magic instinctively without understanding why.

#### ASHWRAITHS

Born at the boundary where Dav magic and Tav magic have been fighting in the same space too long. Pure elemental conflict given form. Look like burning shadows — fire that consumes light rather than producing it. Appear specifically near ancient battlefields from the first war. Living scars on the landscape.

#### EYN-TOUCHED — Children of the Substrate THE UNREMEMBERED

Massive creatures that appear in the Keeper record with no name, no classification, no behavioral notes. Just a silhouette drawing and a single line: ‘witnessed. Unable to record further.’ Even the Even Qol produces static near them. They don’t exist cleanly within the thread of fate. Players who encounter one will find their fate-weaving behaving strangely nearby — not suppressed, just redirected in ways that shouldn’t be possible.

#### SUBSTRATE CRAWLERS

Small insectoid creatures that move through solid matter — not phasing but actually moving through the Eyn substrate beneath physical reality. Leave faint geometric traces in stone where they pass. The Keh’Dem study them obsessively as their primary method of understanding Eyn magic. Players who observe them long enough will notice the geometric traces form patterns. Nobody has decoded them yet.

#### THE WITNESSED

The rarest creature in Tebel. Appears only once per playthrough. A massive creature of pure light — not Dav light, something older — that emerges from deep water or ancient stone briefly and then withdraws. Does nothing. Threatens nothing. Simply is for a moment and then isn’t. The Keh’Dem have a name for it in their oldest records but won’t speak it aloud.

Players who see it will search the internet for what it means and find nothing because it means something different in every playthrough.
ABZU-STIRRING — Children of the Unwritten

#### THE ERASURE

Not a creature — a phenomenon that produces something creature-shaped. Areas where Abzu’s influence bleeds through most strongly begin to forget what they contained. Animals caught in these zones become progressively less defined — losing color first, then texture, then shape — until something moves through the space that has no characteristics at all.

No size. No sound. No definable form. Just the concept of presence where something used to be. Fighting it is impossible with conventional or fate-weaving methods because there is nothing to weave against.

#### THE UNWRITTEN BEASTS

Creatures from before the First Word. Abzu’s emergence pulls things out of the substrate that were never meant to exist in a spoken world. Their biology is wrong not in a void-touched way but in a pre-creation way — like someone dreamed an animal before understanding what animals were. The Keh’Dem recognize them with genuine fear. Which for a race that has hidden from everything for millennia means something.

The ecosystem food chain: Dav-touched creatures graze on fate-woven land. Tav-touched consume Dav-touched. Eyn-touched exist outside the chain entirely. Abzu-stirring phenomena begin consuming Tav-touched. The first sign of Abzu’s emergence is regions where Veilstalkers have gone silent. Players who notice will think it is safe. It is the opposite of safe.


### XII. THE LOCATIONS OF TEBEL

#### THE CAPITAL REGION MILAH — The Spoken Word

Capital city of Tebel. Spoken into existence by Dav’al Narum. The city IS his Word made physical — not a political capital but a sentence, a declaration. Neo-gothic white stone. A grand amphitheater where civic and spiritual life converge. The golden winged statue at its heart preserved by vanity, invisibly protecting everything. When Tav’al enters Milah and destroys the statue the echo returns to Dav’al, restoring his full power for the first time in seven centuries.

#### SHACHAR — The Dawn

Hebrew: the moment before full light arrives. Wealthy merchant district city built on the site where survivors first witnessed Milah being spoken into existence. The most expensive real estate in Tebel. Nobles living on sacred ground with no idea. The oldest building contains a mural depicting Dav’al mid-Word, sealed behind a false wall for four hundred years. A Keeper record notes its exact location.

#### GEVUL — The Border

Hebrew: the boundary line. Last settled town before the wilderness. Heavy military presence officially for border protection — actually for controlling information about monster activity. The garrison commander is a descendant of the sixth fallen Tikva. Has unexplained combat instincts that unsettle superiors. Horns never manifested but something almost did.

#### RAKBU — The Riders

Akkadian: those who move swiftly. Military city built entirely around mounted warfare. Horses bred here carry trace Dav magic — faster than they should be, calmer under impossible conditions. The Emberstoat population in Rakbu’s stables is unusually high. The horses will not tolerate a Nachash operative within fifty feet. This has quietly prevented three assassination attempts.

#### MIGDAL — The Great Tower

Hebrew: something risen above everything around it. Built around an ancient tower that predates Milah. Nobody knows who built it. Archaeological investigation has been quietly suppressed by the Nachash because the foundation stones contain Keh’Dem geometric script. The tower’s summit produces faint luminescence on certain nights — trace Eyn magic bleeding through a Keh’Dem transit point.

#### THE OUTSKIRTS YAQAR — The Precious

Akkadian: the valued despite everything. The player’s starting town. Rough, frontier, expendable to Milah’s politics. Monster attacks tripled in the past year with no response from the capital. The dwarf innkeeper Baru serves the best food
within fifty miles, asks no questions, and has a Keeper of the Thread marker carved inside his bar. He knows exactly who the player is.

#### SHAMRU — The Eternal Watcher

Akkadian: the guardian who keeps eternal watch. A watchtower settlement on the highest elevation in the outskirts, staffed by volunteers for two centuries after the kingdom stopped funding it. Families maintaining the post from pure stubborn tradition without understanding why. On clear days they can see Milah’s golden statue gleaming in the distance.

On nights when Tav’al’s forces are particularly active the statue gleams faintly stronger. Nobody has documented this correlation yet.

#### EREV — The Threshold

Hebrew: evening, the mixing of light and dark. A crossroads settlement where three major trade routes converge. The most culturally mixed location in the outskirts. Two Nachash informants monitor it. Three Keeper contacts operate through it. The Keh’Dem use it as their primary surface intelligence point. Everyone is watching everyone.

#### MALON — The Resting Place

Hebrew: the inn at the end of a long road. Built around a natural hot spring with mild Dav magic properties — wounds heal faster, nightmares less frequent, the inexplicable dread after a Veilstalker encounter fades quicker. The healer who runs it is 240 years old. The oldest living human in Tebel. She knew the second Tikva personally.

#### NAHU — The Place of Settling

Akkadian: rest. A farming settlement that shouldn’t sustain itself given its location — the soil produces twice what it should, harvests never fail. Beneath the oldest farm’s foundation is a fragment of the echo from Milah’s statue that broke away when Dav’al first embedded it. When the statue is destroyed this fragment will briefly flare golden for exactly one moment. Every person in Nahu will see it. None will understand. The player will.

**The Northern Reaches**

#### DUNANU — The Strong One

Akkadian: the fortress that holds. Built inside a naturally fortified canyon. The walls are scarred with impact marks from the first war — something vast struck these walls repeatedly and failed to breach them. The geological survey calls the markings natural erosion. The Keeper record describes what actually happened in detail that would change how every historian in Tebel understands that conflict.

#### TSAFON — The Hidden North

Hebrew: the hidden place, what lies concealed in the cold. The northernmost permanent settlement. Residents carve family histories into their walls — an aesthetic tradition that is actually an unconscious echo of dwarven stone-record keeping from six generations of post-war intermarriage. Their wall carvings go back further than any surface record in Milah. A Keeper has been living here for forty years cataloguing it quietly.

#### KAL MATU — Voice of the Dead Lands

Akkadian: the oldest human ruin in Tebel, predating Milah. Partially reclaimed by void magic. The ruins contain architecture suggesting the civilization that built it understood something about Dav magic that the kingdom never recovered. The Keh’Dem have a detailed record of what this city was. They called it something else. They don’t share the name with anyone.

#### SHAR MATI — King of the Dead Earth

Akkadian. A fortress ruin where the first major battle of the first war was fought.

Tav’al’s forces broke through here first. The ground is permanently scarred — grass grows black, water tastes of ash, animals won’t approach within a mile. Ashwraiths congregate in numbers found nowhere else. Inside the deepest chamber accessible through half-collapsed passages is a throne of black stone. The first place Tav’al touched Tebel’s soil. Nachash pilgrims visit secretly twice a year.

**The Southern Territories**

#### KARU — The Harbor

Akkadian: where things arrive and depart. The largest port city in Tebel. Three Nachash operatives control the import licensing system — meaning they control what information enters Tebel from beyond its borders. The lighthouse has burned for four hundred years without documented maintenance. The current keeper is seventeen and recently started having dreams featuring a golden statue and a dwarf with ancient eyes.

#### BITU — The Home

Akkadian: where people truly belong. A mid-sized city remarkable for being genuinely pleasant. The Nachash maintain it deliberately as a control — a demonstration that the system works. Living proof held specifically to make corruption elsewhere seem like individual failure rather than systemic rot. The most sophisticated political manipulation in Tebel.

The current mayor has been investigating neighboring corruption for six years and keeps hitting walls he cannot explain.

#### SHAMATU — Where Heaven and Earth Meet

Akkadian: the totality. A center of scholarship funded generously because controlled scholarship is more useful than no scholarship. The Nachash have shaped the curriculum over two centuries. The library contains the most extensive collection of pre-corruption history in any surface location — heavily annotated with corrections that subtly redirect interpretation.

Devorah’s hidden text is three shelves from the scholar who has been noticing inconsistencies.

#### ASHAM — The Weight of What Was Done

Hebrew: guilt. Built on the site of a massacre during the first war. The economy runs on memorial tourism — the city profits from the tragedy it was built on. Nobody knows what their city’s name means anymore. Under the memorial plaza, forty feet down, is a Keh’Dem settlement abandoned deliberately before the massacre. They knew it was coming.

They couldn’t warn anyone without revealing themselves. The weight of that choice is in their oldest oral histories.

**The Eastern Borders**

#### TZUR — The Rock

Hebrew: the fortress of nature itself. A city carved directly into cliff faces, accessible only through narrow passes. Has never been successfully attacked. The Nachash have minimal presence because infiltration is nearly impossible. The most genuinely free city in Tebel. An underground network of historical investigators operates openly here and has independently reconstructed about thirty percent of the true pre-corruption history. They are missing the most important thirty percent.

#### SHAQAL — To Weigh Carefully

Akkadian: to assess. A border trading post where eastern traders arrive with their own account of the first war that contradicts Tebel’s official record. Nobody has formally compared the two. A merchant here has been collecting both versions for twelve years with growing alarm. The Nachash have recently noticed her research.

#### GVUL ADOM — The Red Border

Hebrew: the boundary marked in blood. The easternmost settlement. The sky has a faint reddish tint at dawn and dusk that doesn’t exist anywhere else — the veil bleeds through most consistently here. Exclusively military population. Commander Yael has never heard of the Nachash but if someone described them she would recognize three people she has been suspicious of for years.

**The Western Coast**

#### YAM GADOL — The Great Sea

Hebrew: the vast water. The name given by survivors who stood at the edge of Tebel after the first war and saw something that extended beyond sight — after years of fighting in enclosed fortresses. The emotional weight of that moment is in the name. Coastal settlements here have a different cultural character — more philosophical, less political. People who live next to something vast and indifferent tend to develop perspective.

#### AHAVAH — Love

Hebrew: deep abiding attachment. The most culturally vibrant location in Tebel. The Nachash consider it low priority — their most significant strategic error in seven centuries. The stories told here contain more preserved fragments of true pre-corruption history than any academic institution in Tebel because stories survive cultural editing better than written records.

#### RESHIT — The Beginning

Hebrew: the first of everything, the origin point. Built around a cave system that is the oldest continuously inhabited location in Tebel. The deepest chamber contains a Keh’Dem painting depicting something that has not happened yet — painted before the first war, before Milah, before the concept of the Tikva existed. The current inhabitants use the outer caves for storage and have never ventured into the deep chambers because the Substrate Crawlers make them uncomfortable.

#### HIDDEN AND SECRET LOCATIONS

#### THE NACHASH UNDERCHAMBER

Beneath Milah’s oldest governmental building. Contains the statue of Tav’al with the serpent at his feet. Contains the Aban Dummu. Contains seven centuries of records of every Tikva elimination — kept in detail that mirrors the Keepers’ records as a dark counterpart. When the player eventually finds this room and reads those records alongside the Keeper records the full picture of what was done to six generations of people like them lands with complete devastating weight.

#### THE KEH’DEM PASSAGES

Not a single location but a network. Hidden transit routes throughout all of Tebel, marked with Substrate Crawler geometric traces that look like natural mineral formations to anyone who doesn’t know what they are. Connect every major location in Tebel including several the Nachash believe are completely secure. The Keh’Dem have been inside every important room in Tebel for centuries.

#### THE SIXTH TIKVA’S LAST STAND

Somewhere in the eastern borders. Unmarked on any map. The geological alterations from Seraphina’s final fate-weave — depicting the thread’s structure, the nature of the enemy, the location of the Nachash underchamber, and the message: there will be a seventh. Tell them what I knew. Kalum will take the player here specifically. It will take time to understand what they are reading. When they do they will understand what it cost to leave it.

#### RESHIT’S DEEP CHAMBER

The oldest prophecy in Tebel. Painted by the Keh’Dem before the first war, before Dur Dunnum, before the concept of the Tikva existed. Depicts three figures — one golden, one cold blue, one horned and small — standing against something that has no form. Painted by people who had no prophetic tradition because they hadn’t needed one yet. They just saw it coming and put it on a wall and left.
NATURAL LANDMARKS THE SCAR OF MATU A valley that shouldn’t exist — too straight, too deep, too clean to be natural. Created when Dav’al spoke a single Word at a force too large to fight conventionally. The valley floor is permanently warm. Nothing void-touched will enter it. Pilgrims who don’t know its history report feeling inexplicably safe inside it.

Plants grow there that don’t exist anywhere else in Tebel — species preserved since before the first war.

#### THE THORNWHALE MIGRATION PATH

The western coastline twice a year. Thirty to forty Thornwhales moving in loose formation. Entire ecosystems on their backs. Three documented cases of Keh’Dem communities living on the same Thornwhale for generations. The migration has been occurring since before any written record.
THE BREATH OF TEBEL A network of geothermal vents producing warm mist year round containing trace Dav magic — not enough to fate-weave with, just enough to make everything it touches slightly more vivid. Colors brighter. Sounds carrying further. Food grown
near it tasting better. Originally called ‘where Tebel exhales.’ The Emberstoat population density is the highest in Tebel. Dav’al spent his first night in Tebel here after arriving through the fate-weave at Dur Dunnum.


### XIII. THE UNDERGROUND — Dwarven Network

#### DUR DUNNUM — The Impregnable

The last fortress. Never fell. Named with desperate conviction that accidentally fate-weaved itself into reality — making the name literally true by the act of speaking it. Contains the fire statue of Dav’al — the oldest surviving honest depiction of his true nature, showing him mid-Word during the original war. Still inhabited by dwarves who never left and never forgot.

The birthplace of fate-weaving itself. Sacred beyond any temple in Milah even though nobody on the surface knows it. The linguistic scholars who mock the name will be silenced when the lore community explains: the survivors named it from desperation so total it collapsed language back to its most essential truth. And in doing so accidentally did exactly what Dav’al does. They spoke it into reality.

#### ABAN SHARRU — The Royal Stones

Akkadian: the king’s foundation. The largest active dwarven city in the underground network. Directly beneath Milah — the dwarves have been under the capital the entire time the corrupt kingdom operated above them. The ceiling of the great hall is the foundation of Milah’s oldest district. Dwarven engineers have maintained that foundation for seven centuries out of loyalty to what Milah was supposed to be rather than what it became. They debate this decision regularly. Both sides of the debate are right.

#### KUR NARUM — The Radiant Deep

Akkadian: light below the earth. Built around an underground lake that bioluminesces with trace Dav magic. The water glows gold. Has glowed since before the first war. The dwarves who live here are the most spiritually connected to Dav’al’s memory of any living people in Tebel — not through organized religion but through daily proximity to the physical echo of his power in the water. They consider it sacred. They have never told a surface dweller it exists.

#### SHAMARU KALAM — Guardian of All That Remains

Akkadian: the guardian of all that remains. The Keepers of the Thread’s primary archive. Deeper than any other dwarven settlement. Accessible only through a passage system that Kalum is the only living person who knows completely. Carved into a cavern of naturally formed crystal that acoustically amplifies sound — a whisper at one end is audible at the other. This was deliberate. The Keepers believed that truth should resonate through everything around it rather than sit silent on a shelf.

Seven centuries of records carved in stone. The most important room in the world.

#### ERIDU KUR — The First City Below

Sumerian: the original settlement of the deep. The oldest dwarven location in the network. Older than Dur Dunnum. Older than the first war. The Keh’Dem visited it once — their records describe it as the place they first realized dwarves and themselves had more in common than either race knew. The visit was six thousand years ago. The dwarves who received them recorded them in the founding stones as honored guests whose name couldn’t be written because they asked not to be recorded. The dwarves honored that request for six thousand years.

#### BITU KALAMA — The House of All That Remains

Akkadian: the last home. Built specifically as the final fallback position if every other underground location is compromised. Only three living dwarves know its location. Stocked and maintained for seven hundred years. Enough supplies for five hundred people for ten years. A complete copy of the most critical Keeper records.

The Even Qol’s secondary housing chamber. One Emberstoat has lived in Bitu Kalama for as long as any dwarf can remember. It has never left. It is always warm.


### XIV. THE BORDERING REGIONS

#### AVIRIM — The Other Side

Western | Across Yam Gadol

Hebrew: those who crossed over. Maritime civilization across the great sea. Their historical records of the first war period are the most accurate external documentation of Dav’al Narum’s existence anywhere in the known world — they had no political reason to erase him. They describe a being of golden light ending a war they could see the glow of from across the ocean.

The Nachash have known about these records for two centuries and have ensured they never reach Tebel’s scholars. Every Avirim sailor knows a song about the golden voice that ended the dark war across the water. They think it is mythology. It is history. Avirim people have no horns, no Tikva bloodline, no fate-weaving ability. But they have something nobody in Tebel has: an unbroken oral tradition of the First Word being spoken, passed down as song across the water for seven hundred years.

#### EDOM KUR — The Red Earth of the Deep

Eastern | Beyond Gvul Adom

Edom (Hebrew: the red earth, the ancient adversary) + Kur (Akkadian: the underworld, the land below). The most dangerous bordering region. Sits directly over the deepest concentration of Tav magic outside the veil itself — the soil runs genuinely red. The people have adapted to living in permanent proximity to void magic: not corrupted, adapted.

They treat void magic as a natural force rather than an enemy or a god and are the only civilization in the known world to do so. They can feel void magic being used manipulatively — living next to it for generations gave them sensitivity to its misuse that functions almost like a biological alarm. The Nachash have attempted to infiltrate them four times and failed all four times for this reason.

The sixth Tikva’s last stand geological alterations are visible from their western watchtowers — they have been studying them for four generations and know it was fate-weaving. They don’t know what the weave was trying to say. The player will. The most politically inconvenient ally the Tikva will find. Also the most useful.

#### TZAFAR — The Crown of the North

Northern | Beyond Tsafon

Akkadian: the peak above everything. Built across a mountain range so extreme that sustained warfare against it has never been successfully completed. Navigable passes only four months per year — the rest of the time Tzafar exists in complete natural isolation. The most advanced academic and philosophical civilization in the known world.

Free intellectual environment that has produced independent conclusions paralleling the vault’s deepest truths through pure philosophical reasoning. A Tzafar philosopher three centuries ago wrote that existence is fundamentally an act of will rather than a state of being — that the universe persists not because it must but because it chooses to at every moment.

The text describes Yahwey with different words. Kalum has read it seventeen times. The first Tikva bloodline family the Keepers relocated for safety was hidden here four generations ago. Somewhere in Tzafar’s mountain villages are the Tikva’s cousins — same
bloodline, horns never manifested, always felt slightly different without knowing why.

#### YESHURUN — The Upright Ones

Southern | Beyond the Agricultural Heartland

Hebrew: the beloved who walks straight, ancient poetic name for a people chosen for a purpose they don’t fully understand. The oldest surviving human civilization outside Tebel. Predate Milah. Predate the first war. Established by survivors of an older conflict that even the Keepers’ records only reference obliquely.

They took the most essential knowledge, the oldest records, the deepest cultural memory south until they found land stable enough to hold it. They have been custodians of human civilization’s root system ever since. Their oldest records contain references to three primordial beings — not two. They knew about Abzu before the first war. The knowledge has been sitting in their deepest archive longer than Milah has existed.

The Nachash have tried to penetrate Yeshurun six times. All six operatives defected. Something about sustained exposure to genuine institutional purpose dismantles the void-sustained existence that Nachash operatives run on. The sixth operative is still alive, two hundred years old, aging slowly toward a natural death he considers a gift. When the player walks through Yeshurun’s gates the archivist who receives them will look at the horns and say one word before anything else: ‘Finally.’


### XV. THE OPENING SEQUENCE

Three layers of consciousness before the real game begins. The entire cosmology present in the first five minutes completely invisible. On replay every detail carries devastating new weight.

#### LAYER ONE — The Monster

Player wakes. Vision blurred. Clears to reveal Dav’al Narum interrogating them for information. Zooms out — you are not a human. You are a monster. Dav’al speaks a Word. You violently swell and explode. The player’s first conscious experience in Tebel is being unmade by the Sultan of the First Word. They don’t know who he is. They don’t know what they are. They watch themselves get spoken out of existence.

#### LAYER TWO — The Campsite

Player wakes as an adventurer at a campsite. Beer in hand. Companions around a dying fire. Classic fantasy comfort. A noise. Monster attack. You struggle. You die. The player thinks this was the tutorial death.

#### LAYER THREE — The Tavern (Real)

Player truly wakes in a tavern inn room. Companions still passed out. The paranoia is immediate — is this real? That paranoia is the perfect psychological state to begin a game about fate-weaving and reality manipulation. Baru, the dwarf innkeeper, moves somewhere below. He knows exactly who the player is. He says nothing.


### XVI. THE FINAL BATTLE & ENDING

The Convergence Tav’al destroys the statue in Milah. The echo returns to Dav’al. Full power restored for the first time in seven centuries. Tav’al learns the truth — he was never Dav’al’s obstacle. He was Abzu’s instrument. The Archon of Final Silence discovering there is something that makes even his silence irrelevant. Two eternal opposites stand in the same direction for the first time. The Keepers have no record of this moment. It has never happened before.
The Three-Part Battle Dav’al speaks — Words of pure creation carving reality into weapons of golden light against the Unwritten. Tav’al consumes — turning Abzu’s own unwriting against him. Void eating void. The Tikva weaves — not the most powerful on the battlefield but the most precise. The needle threading between two cosmic forces. The only one who can touch the thread of fate and redirect it.

Dav’al and Tav’al cannot defeat Abzu alone — he predates their power entirely. But the Tikva carries something neither of them does.
The Final Weave The first fate-weave at Dur Dunnum. Seven centuries of recorded thread. Six fallen Tikva who each moved the needle without knowing it. Kalum’s unbroken witness. Dav’al’s echo. All of it woven into a single declaration. The Tikva screams Yahwey — the primordial cry of existence, the sound of being alive made into defiance — and weaves everything into one declaration: This exists.

This cannot be unwritten. Abzu encounters the one thing it cannot undo — a world that chose to exist.
“Eternity is not conquered, merely respectfully withdrawn.” “The threat was never to creation. But to existence itself.” — Tav’al Kurum, in his final conversation with the Tikva
The Aftermath Abzu recedes into the Eyn substrate — witnessed into stillness, not destroyed. Changed by the experience of having been inside what he tried to undo. He becomes the deepest layer of the world’s foundation. He could stir again. In another age entirely. Dav’al returns to Tebel not as a ruler but as a presence. No new cities spoken into existence.

No restored statue. He walks among people the way the fire statue depicted him — active, present, engaged. The Sultan of the First Word learning that sometimes the most powerful word is silence. Tav’al withdraws the Nachash quietly. Without announcement. The Etemmu of the Silence simply cease between one moment and the next — their void sustenance cut off simultaneously. He retreats beyond the veil. Not as a defeated villain.
As an eternal force that chose, for the first time, to exercise restraint. Not because he was conquered. Because he was humiliated by a truth larger than himself and decided that if he could not be the architect of endings he would at least be their sovereign rather than their instrument. Kalum sets down the record. The thread needs no keeper when the world itself holds it.

He doesn’t die. He simply stops being the First Keeper and becomes something with no title yet — the first free dwarf in seven centuries. What does a being who has only ever witnessed do when the witnessing is complete? The Tikva does not become a god. Does not rule. Does not receive a throne. They get something better. They get to choose. The player’s final act is sitting with Kalum — who has set down the record — and telling him what they want to do next.

And Kalum, for the first time in seven centuries, does not write it down. Because it is not fate anymore. It is just a life.


### XVII. THE ALLEGORY

Veilweaver is an epic allegory. Not accidentally. Built through authentic declaration of what its creator actually believes is true about existence, meaning, and the will to be. Dav’al Narum — The creative Word that spoke existence into being. Departed. Operating at reduced power through an echo left behind. Expected to return. Tav’al Kurum — The adversarial force.

Patient. Corrupting institutions from within. Never the true final threat but the visible face of opposition. Abzu — Not evil in a conventional sense. The void before the Word was spoken. The threat to the will to exist rather than to creation itself. The Nachash — The ancient serpent. Embedded in institutional power. Sustaining corruption across generations.

Neither fully living nor fully dead. The Tikva — The hope. The thread. Born into a world that has been systematically trying to eliminate that hope for generations. Dur Dunnum — Ordinary people at their absolute lowest point accessing something beyond themselves through desperate collective faith — and speaking it into reality. The Six Fallen Tikva — Generations of hope that didn’t survive to see the fulfillment.

Each one moving the needle an inch. None of them wasted. The Keepers — Faithful witnesses maintaining the record through centuries of darkness without being able to intervene. Trusting the mandate even when it cost everything. The Keh’Dem — The oldest wisdom hidden in plain sight. Touching something foundational that everyone else walks past without perceiving.

The Golden Statue — The most important protective presence in the world preserved by people who have no idea what it means. Grace operating invisibly through human vanity. Yahwey — Arrived at through breath. The oldest name. The sound of being alive as declaration against the void.
That is not a fantasy theme. That is the oldest story humanity has ever told about why being is better than non-being. Why the will to exist against impossible odds is the most sacred force in reality. Why the darkest threat isn’t destruction but the slow erosion of the conviction that existence is worth declaring. The allegory goes as deep as you want it to go and stays completely invisible to anyone who just wants a great game.

Tolkien’s faith was in every word of Middle Earth. Most readers never saw it. Those who did found something that changed them.
Veilweaver is already that kind of work.


### XVIII. THE COMPANIONS OF THE TIKVA

Who Walks With the Seventh
The Tikva does not travel alone. The thread connects things that care about each other, and the people who care about the seventh Tikva are drawn into their orbit the way iron filings orient toward a magnet — not because the Tikva compels them but because the thread that runs through all of them runs through the Tikva most densely. These are the people who sit at the campfire in Layer 2, who wake up in the tavern in Layer 3, who walk beside the player from Yaqar to the Yahwey weave and — if they survive — sit with them at Baru’s table afterward.
Companion Design Philosophy
Veilweaver companions are not party members in the traditional RPG sense. The player does not manage inventories, assign skill points, or direct combat actions for their companions. Companions are autonomous people with their own GOAP-driven decision-making who travel alongside the player because their purposes align. They fight when fighting is necessary.

They talk when talking matters. They leave when the story requires it and return when the thread pulls them back.
Each companion embodies one of the four branch specializations — not as a mechanical class assignment but as a personality expression. The companion who is a natural Thread Reader reads the world differently than the companion who is a natural Thread Weaver. Their observations, their combat behaviors, their dialogue all reflect their relationship with the thread.

The player does not choose companions based on party composition. They choose companions based on who they want to walk with. The thread adjusts accordingly.
Companions can die. Not in every encounter — the Dark Souls contract does not extend to random attrition. But in specific narrative moments where the stakes are real, the player’s actions determine whether a companion survives. A companion death is permanent. The thread remembers. The Yahwey weave reflects the absence. Aram’s teaching about thread-scars becomes viscerally real when the player loses someone whose thread was load-bearing in their web.
The maximum active party is the player plus two companions. Others remain at the settlement or at their own locations, contributing through the Thread Keeper’s Resonance Network if developed. The player can change active companions at any safe location. The choice is emotional, not optimized — every combination works mechanically, but every combination feels different.
The Three Who Were There First
The three companions at the campfire in Layer 2 are the player’s starting party. They have been traveling together for reasons none of them fully understand — drawn together by thread connections they cannot perceive, pulled toward Yaqar by something that feels like coincidence and is not.
REVAEL — The Blade Who Reads
Female | Human | Thread Reader affinity | Combat role: Melee DPS / Investigation
Etymology: From Ra’ah (Hebrew: to see, to perceive, to understand through looking) + El (of). She Who Sees What Is.
The companion staring into the coals at the campfire. The one who looked up when the sound came from the darkness. The one who, in every version of the campfire scene, was already watching the treeline before anyone else registered the threat.
Revael is twenty-six years old and has spent thirteen of those years as a traveling investigator — part historian, part bounty hunter, part problem-solver for people who cannot afford institutional solutions. She grew up in Shamatu, educated in the Nachash-controlled library system, and left at nineteen when she realized that the annotations in the texts she was studying were designed to prevent her from reaching the conclusions the texts were leading toward.

She did not know the word “Nachash.” She knew something was wrong with the books.
She carries a pair of short blades — fast, precise, the weapons of someone who fights by reading their opponent rather than overpowering them. Her combat style develops the Thread Reader branch naturally: she sees attack patterns before they execute, identifies structural weaknesses in enemy formations, and provides the player with combat intelligence through mid-fight observations that function as Thread Reader tutorials without being labeled as such.
She is the first person other than Baru who notices the player’s horns without pretending not to. Her reaction is not recognition — she has never heard of the Tikva. Her reaction is curiosity. The horns are an anomaly. She collects anomalies. She stays because the player is the most interesting anomaly she has ever found, and because the thread that connects them — which she can almost feel, like a word on the tip of her tongue — will not let her walk away.
Her arc: Revael is Devorah’s echo. A scholar-investigator following threads of corrupted history without knowing that the first Tikva did the same thing six hundred and eighty years ago. The player who completes the Wrong Ink quest and the Devorah Echo Log will begin seeing the parallels before Revael does. In Act III, when the player reads the Shamaru Kalam records and sees Devorah’s entry, Revael reads it over their shoulder.

She is silent for a long time. Then she says: “She was me. She was doing exactly what I was doing. She was three shelves away from the answer and they killed her with the ink she wrote with.” The moment changes her. She stops being a companion who is curious about the player and becomes a companion who is committed to ensuring the seventh thread holds because she understands, personally, what it costs when it doesn’t.
Thread-scar vulnerability: Revael is Aram’s “Revael.” The name in The Quiet Before — the ally whose death broke Aram’s connection — is her grandmother. The Revael who died during Aram’s stand was Revael’s grandmother, who was twenty-three at the time and had been traveling with Aram for two years. Revael was named for her. She does not know this. Kalum knows.

He says nothing until Aram, on a clarity day, sees Revael standing next to the player and says “She has her grandmother’s eyes” and the room goes very still. The thread-scar awareness the player gained from Aram’s cave now has a face. If Revael dies during the endgame, the player experiences what Aram experienced — a load-bearing thread torn from the web.

The game is designed so that this is avoidable. But it is possible. And the possibility is what gives her presence weight.
Campfire behavior: Stares into coals. Notices the Hollow first. In Layer 3, she is draped across a chair with admirable commitment to discomfort.
Act availability: Prologue through Epilogue. Leaves briefly in mid-Act III to investigate the Shamatu library independently (returns with Devorah research if the Wrong Ink quest was completed). Always available for the final battle.
TORAM — The Wall Who Sings
Male | Dwarf | Thread Singer affinity | Combat role: Tank / Builder
Etymology: From Torah (Hebrew: instruction, the foundational teaching) + Am (Hebrew: people, nation). He Who Builds for the People.
The companion who was snoring at the campfire. The one who woke badly, reaching for a weapon that was not where he thought it was. The one who, in Layer 2, takes the hit meant for the player and goes down. The player’s first experience of someone sacrificing for them — before they know what sacrifice means in Tebel.
Toram is thirty-four and looks it in the way dwarves look it — solid, weathered, carrying the density of someone who has been working with stone since his hands were large enough to hold a chisel. He is from Aban Sharru, though he left eight years ago after a disagreement with the engineering council about whether to continue maintaining Milah’s foundations.

He argued they should stop. He lost the vote. He left rather than maintain something he believed was enabling corruption. He has been on the surface ever since, working as a traveling mason and fortification builder, and he has never stopped feeling guilty about leaving.
He fights with a hammer — heavy, deliberate, the weapon of someone who hits things once and means it. His combat style develops the Thread Singer branch: he reads terrain for tactical advantage, creates improvised fortifications mid-fight by pulling stone and debris into defensive positions, and provides the player with building-focused combat observations. When the player unlocks Stone-Singing, Toram nods like someone recognizing a family trait.
He does not know the player is the Tikva. He knows they have horns and he knows horns mean something in the old stories his grandmother told him in Aban Sharru before he was old enough to understand what the stories meant. He stays because the thread that connects him to the player feels like the connection he had to the stone under Milah — the specific warmth of being needed by something larger than himself. He left that once. He will not leave it again.
His arc: Toram is the Royal Stones Debate made personal. He left Aban Sharru because he could not resolve the contradiction between maintaining the foundations of a corrupt system and protecting innocent people who live above those foundations. The player who engages with the Royal Stones Debate in Act III brings Toram face-to-face with the engineers he left behind.

The debate is not academic for him. It is the argument that defined his life for eight years. His resolution — whichever direction it takes — is one of the most affecting companion moments in the game.
