He works with the Tzur historian network, the Yeshurun archivist, and (if the Archivist’s Conscience quest was completed) the reformed Nachash archivist to reconstruct the true history of Tebel.

The work takes the rest of his life. It is the Keeper’s mandate inverted: where he once recorded truth and kept it hidden, he now takes hidden truth and makes it known. The record is no longer carved in stone beneath the world. It is spoken aloud above it.
The Grandfather: Kalum settles in the player’s settlement — or in Yaqar with Baru — and becomes the person he never allowed himself to be: present. Not as a keeper, not as a guide, not as a seven-hundred-year repository of classified information. As an old dwarf who sits by fires and tells stories and lets children climb on him and discovers, six centuries too late, what it feels like to be part of a community rather than its witness.

He teaches. Not fate-weaving — he is not a Tikva. He teaches the thing that seven hundred years of watching taught him: how to see people clearly and love them anyway. The settlement’s children call him grandfather within a year. He pretends to find this presumptuous. He does not correct them.
The Pilgrim: Kalum walks. He has seen everything through the Even Qol and the archive. He has never been anywhere. He walks Tebel’s roads with no mandate, no destination, and no record to maintain. He visits every location the player visited and sees them with his own eyes for the first time — the places he knew only as stone carvings and thread-resonance now experienced as a man standing in them, touching them, smelling the specific air of places he has been watching for centuries.

He sends no reports. He keeps no records. He walks and he looks and he experiences the world he spent seven hundred years documenting as if it were new.
His pilgrim’s journal — not carved in stone, written in ink on paper, impermanent in a way that would have horrified the Keeper — arrives at the player’s settlement in fragments carried by travelers. Brief entries. Observational. Written with the spare precision of someone who has been writing the most important records in the world for seven centuries and is now writing about the taste of apples in a town he never knew existed.
“The bread in Ahavah is better than Baru’s. Do not tell Baru. The penalty for this information reaching him would be severe and I am too old for consequences.”
“Stood at Seraphina’s site today. The geology is exactly as the record describes. The record does not describe the wind. The wind is important. She felt it for nine days. I should have included the wind.”
“Reached Karu. The lighthouse. The flame. Four hundred years of burning. I recorded it seventeen times as a Keeper notation. Standing beneath it is different. The heat is real. The light is real. The family that tends it is real. Records do not capture realness. I knew this. I did not understand it.”
The pilgrim entries become the post-game’s most treasured collectible. The community will assemble them in order. They will find that the route Kalum walks traces, unconsciously, the path the player took through the game — the keeper following the thread one last time, not because the mandate requires it but because the thread is where the people he loved walked, and walking where they walked is the closest thing to company he knows.
On any path: Whatever Kalum becomes, the Even Qol remains with the player. Kalum set it down when he set down the record. It belongs to the Tikva now. The crystal that was the Keeper’s tool for seven hundred years is the Tikva’s birthright for whatever comes next. Kalum, for the first time, carries nothing. His hands are empty. He does not know what to do with empty hands. He is learning.


### XXXV. THE KEH’DEM NEXT ERA — Six Thousand Years and One Morning After

What the Oldest Watchers Do When the Thing They Were Watching For Finally Happens
The Observation Continues (But Different Now)
The Keh’Dem have been watching for six thousand years. Their entire civilization — their culture, their art, their language, their non-religion, their deliberate invisibility — has been shaped by one purpose: observing the web’s structural state and waiting for the moment when both frequencies would be present in stable harmonic. That moment happened.

The Yahwey weave produced it. Tikkun’s observation log entry — “both frequencies detected in stable harmonic” — is the culmination of sixty centuries of patience.
And now the Keh’Dem face the same question Kalum faces: what does purpose become when the purpose is fulfilled?
Their answer is different from Kalum’s because they are different from Kalum. The Keh’Dem are not individuals processing a personal crisis of purpose. They are a civilization processing a species-level transition. And civilizations process transitions the way civilizations process everything: slowly, collectively, and with a lot of arguments that outsiders would find incomprehensible.
The Schism of Visibility
The Keh’Dem’s six-thousand-year survival strategy was invisibility. They removed themselves from the thread of fate. They left no permanent marks. They observed without participating. This strategy was designed for a specific condition: a web that was deteriorating, corrupted by forces that would target the Keh’Dem if they were detected. The strategy worked. Six thousand years of survival is proof.
The condition has changed. The web is healthy. The Nachash are dissolved. The Unwritten has receded. The forces that would have targeted the Keh’Dem no longer exist in their previous form. The strategic justification for invisibility has evaporated.
This produces the first genuine civilizational debate the Keh’Dem have had in four thousand years. The debate has two sides:
The Observers: The faction that believes invisibility should be maintained regardless of external conditions. Their argument: observation requires non-participation. The Keh’Dem’s unique value is their capacity to see clearly because they are not entangled in the outcome. Becoming visible means becoming entangled. Becoming entangled means losing the perspective that made them useful.

The web is healthy now. It will not be healthy forever. The Keh’Dem should remain invisible so that when the next crisis comes — and it will come, because the Unwritten receded but did not disappear — they are positioned to do what they have always done: watch, record, and share an inch of understanding with the person who needs it most.
The Resonants: The faction that believes the rewriting created an opportunity that invisibility wastes. Their argument: the dual-frequency web is new. No one has ever observed a healthy web with both frequencies present. The Keh’Dem’s six thousand years of observation experience, applied to a web that is genuinely functional for the first time, could produce insights that advance the inch of Eyn understanding further than the previous six millennia combined.

But producing those insights requires interaction — experiments that create visible traces, collaborations with surface-dwellers who understand the web from the inside (the player, the Tikva, the Keepers). Remaining invisible in a healthy web is not caution. It is cowardice dressed as tradition.
Tikkun is caught between the two positions. They have been the Resonance — the Keh’Dem who interacts with the surface — for two hundred years. They watched four Tikva. They shared the observation space with the seventh. They are the Keh’Dem most experienced with surface contact and therefore the Keh’Dem most aware of both the value and the danger of visibility.

Their position in the debate is characteristically precise: “The question is not whether to become visible. The question is what we become visible as. A civilization that was hidden for six thousand years does not simply appear. It is revealed. And the nature of the revelation shapes everything that follows.”
The Player’s Role
The Keh’Dem schism is a post-game quest chain triggered by returning to the observation space after the credits. Tikkun is agitated — as agitated as a Keh’Dem gets, which is to say their geometric notation is slightly less precise than usual and they have been pacing, which the player has never seen a Keh’Dem do.
The quest does not ask the player to choose a side. The player is not Keh’Dem and the decision is not theirs. The quest asks the player to provide perspective — to share what they learned about visibility and invisibility across the entire game. The Nachash were invisible and it made them monstrous. The Keepers were invisible and it made them faithful but impotent.

The Thread-Binders were invisible and it kept them alive but marginalized. Dav’al was visible and it made him a target. The golden statue was visible and it protected everything.
The player’s input does not determine the outcome. It influences Tikkun’s position, which influences the debate, which produces one of three results:
Cautious Emergence: The Keh’Dem become visible to specific individuals — the player, the Keepers, select scholars — while maintaining invisibility to the broader world. They establish formal observation exchanges with the Shamaru Kalam archive, combining six thousand years of Keh’Dem geometric records with seven hundred years of Keeper stone records.

The resulting unified record is the most complete account of Tebel’s history that will ever exist. The Keh’Dem remain mysterious to the surface world. Rumors of “small, teal-eyed people” circulate in border settlements. The Keh’Dem consider this acceptable.
Full Emergence: The Keh’Dem reveal themselves to Tebel. The oldest civilization in the world, hidden for six thousand years, walks into the sunlight. The political, cultural, and spiritual consequences are enormous — a people who predate every institution in Tebel, who carry records of events that no one else remembers, who perceive a layer of reality that no one else can see.

The surface world’s reaction ranges from reverence (Yeshurun, who always knew something was in the shadows) to suspicion (the rebuilt Milah government, which has institutional reasons to distrust hidden civilizations) to academic frenzy (the Tzur historians, who immediately request access to everything). The Keh’Dem handle the attention with characteristic understatement.

Tikkun’s first public statement: “We have been here since before your languages had names. We would like to continue being here. Please do not build on the transit points.”
Deepening Withdrawal: The Keh’Dem decide that the healthy web makes deeper observation possible and withdraw further — not into invisibility but into the substrate itself. The Resonant faction’s argument about new observational opportunities is accepted but applied inward rather than outward. The Keh’Dem begin a new phase of Eyn research, using the stable dual-frequency web as a platform for deeper substrate observation than was previously possible.

They become less visible, not more. Tikkun remains as the sole surface contact, now carrying the additional weight of being the only bridge between a civilization that is moving deeper into the foundation of reality and a surface world that will never fully understand what the Keh’Dem are looking for down there.
Each outcome produces different post-game environmental effects: Cautious Emergence creates Keh’Dem-Keeper joint observation posts visible through the enhanced lens. Full Emergence creates Keh’Dem settlements on the surface that the player can visit. Deepening Withdrawal creates zones where the substrate is unusually active — places where the Keh’Dem’s deeper research bleeds through as visible geometric phenomena in the physical world.
Tikkun’s Personal Arc
Regardless of the civilizational outcome, Tikkun undergoes a personal transition. They have been the Resonance for two hundred years — four consecutive cycles of watching Tikva die or break. The seventh held. Tikkun’s assignment is, for the first time, successful.
And Tikkun does not know how to process success. The Keh’Dem are observers. They are trained to record outcomes, not to feel them. But Tikkun watched Malkah govern with love and die in prison. Watched Aram stand at Tier 5 and break. Watched Seraphina carve her death into the landscape. Watched the seventh walk into the observation space carrying the Substrate Crawler Lens and felt, for the first time in two hundred years, something that was not observation.
The player who visits Tikkun regularly in the post-game witnesses the Keh’Dem scout doing something unprecedented: processing an emotional response to an observation. They are not good at it. The Keh’Dem language has no word for “relief” because relief implies investment in an outcome and the Keh’Dem ideal is non-investment. Tikkun is experiencing an emotion they have no vocabulary for, in a culture that considers emotion a form of entanglement, after two hundred years of professional detachment.
The player can help. Not through a quest mechanic — through presence. Visiting. Talking. Sharing what the surface world felt during the reweaving. Giving Tikkun the thing the Keh’Dem philosophy says they should not want: the experience of being part of what they observed. The Thread Keeper bond between the player and Tikkun, if developed, provides the relational framework for Tikkun to understand that observation and participation are not mutually exclusive — that you can watch something and care about it and the caring does not make the watching less precise. It makes the watching worth doing.
Tikkun’s final post-game dialogue, available after sufficient visits and regardless of the civilizational outcome:
“I have been observing for two hundred years. I observed you from Yaqar. I observed the thread you built. I observed the weave you produced. I recorded all of it in geometric notation that will persist in our archives for the next six thousand years.”
A pause. The teal eyes are the same as always. The composure is the same. But the voice carries something new.
“The notation does not capture what it felt like to watch you hold the midpoint. The notation captures the structural event. It does not capture the — the warmth. The notation was not designed for warmth. I am beginning to think this is a limitation of the notation rather than a limitation of the universe.”
Another pause.
“I would like to revise the notation. This will take approximately four hundred years. I will need a collaborator who understands warmth. If you are available.”


### XXXVI. ACCESSORIES — Rings, Necklaces, and Relics

The Worn Thread — Items That Sit Close to the Weaver
Design Philosophy
Accessories in Veilweaver occupy the space closest to the player’s body and, by extension, closest to their thread. Rings sit on fingers that weave. Necklaces rest against the chest where the mana well resonates. Relics — the Veilweaver equivalent of trinkets, idols, and librams — are carried objects of specific devotion or purpose that channel the player’s branch affinity into passive effects.
Unlike weapons and armor, which are tools the player wields, accessories are extensions of the player’s relationship with the thread. A ring does not add damage. It refines how the player’s weaving interacts with the world. A necklace does not add defense. It deepens the player’s connection to a specific aspect of the thread. A relic does not add a new ability. It amplifies an existing conviction.
The player can equip two rings (one per hand), one necklace, and one relic at a time. Swapping is instant and can be done outside of combat. The choice of accessories is as characterful as the choice of weapon — a Thread Reader wearing investigation-focused rings and a lore-keeper’s necklace plays a different game than a Thread Reader wearing combat-awareness rings and a Veilwarden’s pendant.
Accessories follow the same tier system as all equipment: Poor, Good, Superior, Epic, Legendary, Primordial. The Substrate tier has one complete set — one ring pair, one necklace, one relic — that manifests as a unified expression of the deepest layer of reality.

The necklace is the web helping.

#### RELICS

One relic slot. Relics are devotional or purpose-aligned objects that channel the player’s branch affinity into sustained passive effects. Unlike rings and necklaces, which modify the player’s thread interaction, relics express what the player is — their conviction, their alignment, their identity within the web.
Relics are classified by affinity: Codex (Thread Reader), Whetstone (Thread Weaver), Cornerstone (Thread Singer), Bond (Thread Keeper). Each affinity has items at every tier. The player can equip any relic regardless of their branch investment, but relics of matching affinity provide a 25% bonus to their effects.

THE SUBSTRATE SET — One Ring, One Necklace, One Relic
Fate-decided. Never crafted. Never pursued. Encountered by players the substrate considers ready.
The Substrate set is a matched trio. Unlike individual Substrate items (which manifest independently based on specific qualities), the set appears as a complete ensemble — all three pieces simultaneously — under a single manifestation condition that is the rarest trigger in the game.
The condition: the player must have encountered at least three of the five individual Substrate items (The Lens, The Staff, The Ember, The Weight, The Thread) during their playthrough AND must return to the point where the Yahwey weave was performed AND must simply stand there, not weaving, not fighting, not building, not investigating — just existing, in the place where existence was declared, wearing or carrying the Substrate items they have already received.
The substrate responds to the presence of its own fragments gathered in one place by a single person. It stirs. The player feels the vibration below the Eyn frequency — something deeper than the Keh’Dem have ever measured, deeper than the observation space can perceive. For one moment, the player stands at the intersection of every layer of reality: the web above, the substrate beneath, and the deep formless potential beneath that.
The set appears. Not dramatically. Not with light or sound. The items are simply there, as if they had always been there and the player had only now noticed.
The Substrate Ring: Before and After
A ring that appears to be made of nothing. Not invisible — present, occupying space, perceptible to touch — but made of a material that has no color, no texture, no temperature. It is made of potential. Unrealized possibility crystallized into a shape that happens to be a ring because the player’s hands are what the substrate knows best about them.
Effect: The player’s fate-weaving operates one tier higher than their actual tier. A Tier 5 weaver weaves at Tier 6 effective power. A Tier 6 weaver weaves at Tier 7 — Yahwey-level — at will, without the density requirements of the original weave. The ring does not add power. It removes the distinction between what the player can do and what the player could do.

Potential and actual become the same thing. The substrate, which is nothing but potential, provides the bridge.
The Substrate Necklace: The Sound Before the Word
A necklace that hums at a frequency below any Thread Sense category. Not Dav warmth. Not Tav silence. Not Keh’Dem geometry. Not Abzu absence. The sound that existed before any of these distinctions were meaningful — the hum of undifferentiated potential before it differentiated into creation and void.
Effect: The player is immune to all frequency-based effects — positive and negative. Dav magic does not enhance them. Tav magic does not harm them. Eyn substrate does not disorient them. They exist at the frequency of the foundation itself, which is the frequency that all other frequencies are built on. This is not power. It is position. The player stands at the bottom of everything and nothing can reach below them because there is no below.
The Substrate Relic: The First Decision
A small, smooth stone that is warm in a way that predates the concept of warmth. Carrying it, the player feels what Abzu felt at the moment of the Sundering — not the grief (that is The Weight of Before’s domain) but the decision. The moment when undifferentiated wholeness became differentiated reality. The moment when existence chose to exist. The First Decision is not Dav’al’s Word.

It is the thing that made the Word possible — the substrate’s own choice to allow differentiation, to permit the emergence of its children even though the emergence would exile it.
Effect: Once per playthrough, the player can make a decision that the game recognizes as absolute. A single choice — any choice — that cannot be undone by any mechanism, any weave, any power. Not reversible through New Game Plus. Not editable through Causal Rethread. The decision stands in the substrate itself, as permanent as the Sundering. The player chooses once, about anything, and the choice becomes part of the foundation of reality.
The game never prompts this use. The player must discover — through experimentation or intuition — that The First Decision allows them to do this. When they use it, the confirmation dialogue is a single line:
“This cannot be undone. The substrate will remember.”
What the player chooses to make permanent says more about them than any gameplay metric could capture. Some will use it to save a companion permanently. Some will use it to make a structure indestructible. Some will use it to ensure a political outcome survives into future content. Some will carry The First Decision for the entire post-game and never use it because they understand that the most powerful expression of choice is sometimes the choice not to use the power you have.
The community will debate what to use it on for years. The debates will be more interesting than any answer.


### XXXVII. TABARDS AND THE REPUTATION SYSTEM

Wearing Your Thread — Faction Alignment as Visible Commitment
Design Philosophy
Tabards in Veilweaver are not cosmetic. They are declarations. Wearing a faction’s tabard is a public statement: I stand with these people. The thread records it. The web responds. NPCs react. Reputation accrues.
The reputation system operates on the Root Pillar — relationships deepened through consistent demonstrated alignment. Wearing a tabard does not instantly make you trusted. It makes your actions visible to the faction you represent, and their assessment of you deepens based on what you do while wearing their colors. A player who wears the Veilwarden tabard and fights void-touched creatures gains Veilwarden reputation.

A player who wears the Veilwarden tabard and avoids combat loses it. The tabard is a promise. The actions are the proof.
The Tabard Slot
Tabards occupy a dedicated equipment slot (separate from armor). They are visible over whatever armor the player wears — a visual layer that declares allegiance. Only one tabard can be worn at a time. Switching tabards is instant but switching while in a faction-aligned area is noticed by NPCs (“Changed your colors, have you?” is a line the player will hear exactly as many times as they deserve).
Faction Reputation Tiers
Each faction has five reputation levels. Progression is not linear — early tiers advance quickly through basic alignment actions; later tiers require specific meaningful contributions.
Tier
Name
General Threshold
1
Acknowledged
Wear the tabard. Be seen. The faction knows you exist.
2
Trusted
Consistent aligned actions while wearing the tabard. The faction relies on you for routine contributions.
3
Honored
Significant quest completions or sustained contribution. The faction considers you a core member.
4
Revered
Major faction-specific achievements. The faction’s leadership respects you personally.
5
Exalted
The faction considers you foundational to their identity. You are not a member. You are part of what they are.

Each reputation tier unlocks faction-specific rewards: equipment, crafting recipes, building templates, lore access, and — at Exalted — a faction-specific Legendary accessory.
The Factions and Their Tabards
Keepers of the Thread
Tabard: Deep stone-grey with a silver thread pattern woven through the fabric. The pattern is subtle — visible in certain light, invisible in others. The Keepers’ nature expressed as clothing.
Reputation actions: Recording observations (using Aurath Crystal to document thread activity), discovering lore, completing investigation quests, maintaining the stone archives, visiting the Shamaru Kalam regularly. The Keepers value the act of witnessing. Every observation made while wearing their tabard is noted.
Tier
Reward
Acknowledged
Access to Keeper waypoints (underground fast-travel network)
Trusted
Keeper’s Cloak — +15% Pattern Recall clarity, Keeper NPCs share additional lore details
Honored
Shamaru Kalam deep archive access — additional vault lore entries unavailable elsewhere
Revered
Stone-Carving technique — player can create permanent environmental records (lore markers visible to other players in future multiplayer)
Exalted
Kalum’s Mandate (Legendary Codex Relic) — “Witness and record.” All Pattern Recall echoes are now permanently recordable. The player’s observations become part of the stone archive. What you see, the Keepers keep.

Veilwardens
Tabard: Dark red-brown with the eastern border’s crimson horizon stitched across the chest. Practical fabric. Stain-resistant. The Veilwardens design for function.
Reputation actions: Killing void-touched creatures, completing border patrol routes, defending settlements, assisting Veilwarden garrisons, providing intelligence on Nachash movement. The Veilwardens value demonstrated combat commitment to the border. Talk is cheap. The tabard asks you to fight.
Tier
Reward
Acknowledged
Veilwarden supply access — military-grade consumables available at garrison merchants
Trusted
Veilwarden Combat Training — +10% damage vs void-touched (stacks with existing bonuses), access to Warden patrol routes as fast-travel
Honored
Commander’s Intelligence Briefings — eastern border threat maps update in real-time on the player’s enhanced lens
Revered
Veilwarden Officer Commission — player can direct garrison patrols to specific locations, redirecting military resources
Exalted
The Red Horizon (Legendary Whetstone Relic) — “The border holds.” While worn, the player’s combat abilities function at +20% effectiveness when fighting within 30m of any settlement, ally, or structure they are protecting. The Veilwardens protect. The relic rewards protecting.

Thread-Binders
Tabard: Unbleached linen with forearm-extending sleeves that display the wearer’s Thread-Binder tattoo pattern. Players without Thread-Binder heritage receive a temporary geometric mark when they first equip the tabard — the thread acknowledging their commitment even without the bloodline.
Reputation actions: Teaching Thread-Binders about fate-weaving (sharing branch knowledge with their community), identifying Nachash infiltration in civilian areas, protecting Thread-Binder families from persecution, conducting weaving demonstrations that inspire hidden sensitives to acknowledge their abilities. The Thread-Binders value the spread of thread-awareness to ordinary people.
Tier
Reward
Acknowledged
Thread-Binder safe houses — hidden rest points in every major city
Trusted
Network Intelligence — Thread-Binder informants flag suspicious activity in the player’s visited locations
Honored
Tattoo Resonance — the player’s temporary mark becomes permanent and functional: +10% weave efficiency in areas with Thread-Binder presence
Revered
Thread-Binder Recruitment — the player can identify latent thread-sensitives and guide them to the network, expanding the faction’s reach
Exalted
The People’s Thread (Legendary Bond Relic) — “The thread runs through everyone.” While worn, every NPC within 20m gains a faint passive thread-connection to the player. Crowd areas become awareness zones. The thread-sensitivity that the Thread-Binders preserved for centuries, extended to everyone nearby through the player’s presence.

The Resonance (Keh’Dem)
Tabard: Not fabric. A geometric pattern that the player wears on their skin — a temporary Keh’Dem notation applied by Tikkun that marks the wearer as “observed and accepted.” It glows faintly teal in substrate-active areas.
Reputation actions: The Keh’Dem do not value action. They value quality of observation. Reputation increases when the player examines substrate phenomena carefully, uses the Substrate Crawler Lens with precision, correctly interprets Keh’Dem geometric notation, and — crucially — refrains from acting when observation is more appropriate than intervention. The Keh’Dem reward patience. Rushing costs reputation.
Tier
Reward
Acknowledged
Substrate Trace Visibility — Keh’Dem transit markers glow more brightly, easier to navigate
Trusted
Observation Exchange — Tikkun shares weekly substrate readings through the third frequency, providing environmental intelligence unavailable through any other faction
Honored
Geometric Notation Fluency — the player’s partial notation literacy (40% from Movement II) increases to 70%. More Keh’Dem records become readable
Revered
Transit Network Access — full access to Keh’Dem passages as a fast-travel network across all of Tebel
Exalted
The Inch (Legendary Codex Relic) — “Six thousand years. One inch. Observation continues.” While worn, the player perceives one additional layer of reality beneath whatever they are currently examining. Investigation reveals substrate-level information. The Keh’Dem shared an inch of understanding with the Tikva. The relic carries that inch forward.

First Word Church
Tabard: White linen with a golden sunburst pattern centered on the chest. The official vestment of lay members. Wearing it in Milah grants access to Church facilities and services.
Reputation actions: The Church is complex — it is wrong about fate-weaving but genuine about its faith. Reputation increases through acts of genuine service (healing, protection, community support), respectful engagement with Church theology (the player can disagree without being dismissive), and — at higher tiers — careful theological correction that guides the Church back toward its original truth without destroying its institutional faith.

The reputation system rewards the player who treats the Church as a wounded institution worth healing rather than an enemy worth destroying.
Tier
Reward
Acknowledged
Church sanctuary access — rest points in every city with a Church presence, minor healing services
Trusted
Clergy contacts — Church officials provide intelligence on civic matters and Nachash-influenced theological positions
Honored
Theological Dialogue — the player can engage in formal theological discussions that gradually shift Church doctrine. Each successful dialogue moves the institution fractionally toward the truth about fate-weaving.
Revered
Reformer Status — the Church officially acknowledges that the Tikva’s horns are not a curse. Publicly. This changes NPC reactions across all Church-influenced territories.
Exalted
The Corrected Text (Legendary Codex Relic) — “The thread is a gift.” A rewritten Church theological text that the player helped produce — correcting three centuries of Nachash editorial drift. While worn: all Church-affiliated NPCs treat the player as spiritually authoritative. Theological discussions auto-succeed. The Church’s institutional faith, no longer weaponized against the thread, becomes a source of genuine Root power: +15% to all Root-aligned effects in Church-influenced territories.

Dwarven Underground (Aban Sharru Council)
Tabard: Heavy woven fabric with stone-dust infused into the fiber. The color of deep earth. Wearing it underground feels like wearing the mountain.
Reputation actions: Building (especially underground or with dwarven materials), structural maintenance, engineering contributions, participating in the ongoing Royal Stones Debate, and — critically — acts that bridge the surface-underground divide. The dwarves value anyone who builds with conviction and maintains what they build.
Tier
Reward
Acknowledged
Dwarven market access — superior crafting materials, building templates, and engineering tools
Trusted
Engineering Mentorship — dwarven engineers provide building feedback that improves Stone-Singing effectiveness by 10%
Honored
Foundation Access — the player can access and use the maintenance tunnel network beneath Milah independently
Revered
Master Builder recognition — all player-built structures gain a passive “dwarven reinforcement” bonus: +20% structural integrity
Exalted
The Unbroken Foundation (Legendary Cornerstone Relic) — “Seven centuries of keeping their buildings standing. It wasn’t for nothing.” While worn: player-built structures never degrade. Environmental damage, void-seepage, even Unwriting — the foundation holds. The seven centuries of dwarven maintenance, applied as an eternal principle to everything the player builds.

Reputation Mechanics
Passive Accrual: Actions aligned with a faction’s values generate small reputation gains while wearing their tabard. Killing void-touched creatures in the Veilwarden tabard: +1 rep per kill. Recording an observation in the Keeper tabard: +2 rep per entry. Building a structure in the Dwarven tabard: +3 rep per completion. The drip is constant. Commitment compounds.
Quest Bonuses: Completing quests that align with a faction’s interests while wearing their tabard grants significant reputation bonuses. The Gvul Adom Red Sky Patrol in Veilwarden colors: +500 rep. The Wrong Ink quest in Keeper colors: +300 rep. The bonus applies retroactively if the player returns to a completed quest location while wearing the relevant tabard and performs a brief thread-recording of what they did there.
Decay: Reputation does not decay from inactivity. The thread remembers. Once earned, reputation is permanent. However, actions against a faction’s values while wearing their tabard cause reputation loss. Attacking a Church NPC in Church colors: significant loss. Ignoring a void-touched attack on a settlement in Veilwarden colors: moderate loss. The tabard is a promise. Breaking it costs.
Multi-Faction Play: The player can build reputation with all factions across a playthrough by switching tabards. There is no faction exclusivity. The thread connects everything — the player who helps the Veilwardens AND the Keepers AND the Church is not contradicting themselves. They are being the Tikva, which means holding all the threads. The Exalted tier for every faction simultaneously is achievable and is recognized by the game: NPCs in the post-game reference the player’s multi-faction standing with a mixture of respect and the specific awe reserved for someone who managed to make everyone happy without lying to anyone.
“The thread remembers what you wore. The web knows who you stood with. The tabard is not a costume. It is a conviction made visible.”


### XXXVIII. THE COMPLETE ARMORY EXPANSION

New Weapon Categories, Armor Slots, and Named Sets

#### ARMORY OVERVIEW

The existing Item Catalogue covers ten weapon categories and three armor weight classes with six items each (Poor through Primordial). This expansion adds four new weapon categories, eight individual armor slot types, and sixteen named armor sets with multi-piece bonuses. Combined with the existing catalogue and the accessories system from Section XXXVI, the Complete Armory provides the full equipment ecosystem for Veilweaver.
Existing (retained from Item Catalogue): Blades, Hammers & Maces, Staves, Gauntlets, Shields, Shortbows, Longbows, Recurve War Bows, Compound Crossbows, Light/Medium/Heavy chest armor.
New weapon categories: Axes & Cleavers, Spears & Polearms, Daggers, Off-Hand Weaving Focuses.
New armor slots: Headpieces (Horn-Crowns), Shoulders, Bracers, Gloves, Belts, Legguards, Boots, Cloaks.
Named sets with bonuses: Sixteen curated sets spanning all tiers and archetypes.
Total equipment count after expansion: 400+ unique named items.

#### NEW WEAPON CATEGORIES

Axes & Cleavers
The axe is the weapon of decisive separation. Where blades slice with precision, axes commit — heavy, final, the strike that does not redirect but severs. Thread Weaver affinity: Thread Blade delivered through an axe strike severs thread connections with a wider area of effect, making axes the premier weapon for fighting groups of void-touched creatures whose thread connections sustain each other.

The visual of a golden thread-arc following an axe swing is one of the most satisfying combat animations in the game.
Item
Tier
Stats
Effect
Source
Woodsman’s Hatchet
Poor
DMG: 15 SPD: Moderate STM: 7/swing
Basic cleave. Hits two enemies in arc. No thread resonance.
General merchants
Frontier Cleaver
Good
DMG: 25 SPD: Moderate STM: 8/swing
Cleave arc widened 20%. +8% damage to Hollow creatures. The frontier answer to animals that shouldn’t still be moving.
Yaqar / Gevul smiths
Veil-Severing Axe
Superior
DMG: 38 SPD: Moderate STM: 9/swing
Thread Weaver Affinity: Thread Blade cleave severs connections across a 3m arc. Void-touched groups lose coordination when struck. Cold-damage bonus vs Nachash.
Crafting: Dwarven Alloy + Void-Blood
The Archivist’s Cleave
Epic
DMG: 48 SPD: Moderate STM: 8/swing
Pattern Recall on kill: brief echo of the severed connection shows what the enemy was connected to. Investigation through violence — Revael’s preferred combination. Thread Reader Affinity: +20% echo clarity on killed targets.
Nachash Underchamber (Operative armory, Act V)
Gideon’s Splitting Maul
Legendary
DMG: 60 SPD: Moderate STM: 7/swing
Stand Your Ground: damage increases 5% per second of continuous combat without retreating (max +35%). Cleave arc generates temporary cover for allies behind the player. Gideon held the line. The axe remembers what holding looks like.
Gideon Echo Log complete + Healer’s guidance to the battlefield cache
The Severance
Primordial
DMG: 75 SPD: Moderate STM: 6/swing
Dual Frequency: cleave severs both Dav and Tav connections simultaneously. Void-touched targets hit lose void-sustenance permanently — they begin dying naturally. Unwriting zones struck by the cleave arc partially re-solidify. The axe that cuts reality clean.
Forge: Seraphina’s Stand. Requires having severed a Nachash operative’s sustenance + Harmonic Residue + Seraphina’s Stand Stone

Spears & Polearms
Reach weapons. The spear keeps the enemy at distance and the thread at arm’s length — a philosophical distinction that makes spears the preferred weapon of Thread Readers who want observation space between themselves and the thing they are observing. Thread Reader affinity: the spear’s reach extends Thread Sense into a focused cone along the weapon’s line, turning every thrust into a simultaneous attack and investigation. The player who fights with a spear reads enemies in the act of killing them.
Item
Tier
Stats
Effect
Source
Militia Pike
Poor
DMG: 18 SPD: Slow STM: 7/swing
Long reach. Keeps enemies at distance. The honest tool of people who want to stay alive.
General merchants
Warden’s Halberd
Good
DMG: 28 SPD: Slow STM: 8/swing
Reach +20%. Thread Sense extends along the weapon: +10% detection range in the direction the spear points. The border guards learn to feel threats through their weapons.
Gvul Adom garrison armory
Thread-Point Lance
Superior
DMG: 40 SPD: Slow STM: 9/swing
Thread Reader Affinity: thrust attacks trigger Pattern Recall on the target — brief combat echo revealing the enemy’s last three actions. Charged thrust: 2x damage + full enemy thread profile visible for 5 seconds.
Crafting: Dwarven Alloy + Aurath Antler Crystal tip
Kalum’s Walking Staff (Combat Form)
Epic
DMG: 35 SPD: Moderate STM: 6/swing
Seven centuries of walking. The staff knows every road in Tebel. Reach +30%. All four Thread Sense frequencies enhanced +15% along the weapon line. On charged thrust: target’s complete thread history visible for 10 seconds. Kalum did not fight with this staff. He read the world through it. Now it reads for you.
Kalum (given during Act III if player demonstrates Thread Reader investment)
The Witness
Legendary
DMG: 50 SPD: Moderate STM: 5/swing
The Keeper’s weapon. On kill: the enemy’s thread history is permanently recorded in the player’s Echo Log — a complete account of what the creature was before void corruption. Charged thrust against a boss: reveals the boss’s entire thread profile, all vulnerabilities, all connections. The weapon does not just kill. It remembers what it killed.
Shamaru Kalam deep archive (hidden chamber, requires full Keeper reputation)
Eyn-Touched Polearm
Primordial
DMG: 62 SPD: Moderate STM: 4/swing
Dual Frequency: the spear’s reach extends into the substrate. Attacks affect enemies slightly before they arrive — the spear strikes where the enemy will be because it reads their thread forward in time. Cannot miss targets whose thread is visible. The weapon that observed its way into the foundation.
Forge: Kur Narum Lake. Requires Thread Reader Mastery + Harmonic Residue + Living Crystal + Substrate Crawler-traced stone

Daggers
Speed weapons. Paired daggers are the fastest weapon type in the game and the premier stealth option. Thread Suppression affinity: daggers dampen the player’s thread signature per strike rather than amplifying it, making each kill quieter rather than louder. The stealth archer has the bow. The stealth assassin has daggers. Both are Thread Reader primary builds; the difference is range versus certainty.

Tier
Stats
Effect
Source

Paired. 4-hit combo. Fast and forgettable. Everyone carries one.
General merchants

Paired. 5-hit combo. +15% damage from stealth. Thread Suppression: each kill from stealth reduces thread signature for 3 seconds.

Paired. 5-hit combo. +30% damage from stealth. Thread Suppression sustained: kills from stealth extend suppression by 5 seconds (stacking). Void-touched targets killed silently do not alert others within 10m.

Devorah’s Quills

DMG: 18 SPD: Very Fast STM: 2/swing
Paired. 6-hit combo. +40% from stealth. Thread Reader Affinity: Pattern Recall activates on stealth kill — the target’s last hour of activity visible as echo. Investigation daggers: kill something quietly, learn everything it knew. Devorah’s letter openers, reforged for someone who finishes investigations differently than she did.
Wrong Ink quest chain completion + Devorah vignette
The Silence Before
Legendary
DMG: 26 SPD: Very Fast STM: 1/swing
Paired. 7-hit combo. +60% from stealth. Thread Suppression mastery: the player’s thread signature is completely invisible for 10 seconds after any stealth kill. During this window, all actions (including weaving) produce zero thread disturbance. The silence that existed before the Word — weaponized not as void but as privacy.
Keh’Dem observation space (hidden, requires Tikkun’s trust + geometric puzzle that tests whether the player can be quiet)
Paradox
Primordial
DMG: 32 SPD: Very Fast STM: 1/swing
Paired. Dual Frequency: the daggers exist in both creation and void simultaneously. Strikes against void-touched targets deal creation damage. Strikes against Dav-touched targets deal void damage. Strikes against neutral targets deal both. The daggers choose which frequency to express based on what will be most effective. Post-Yahwey: stealth kills with Paradox heal the web at the kill location. Death becomes repair.
Manifestation: appears for stealth-archetype players who completed the game with maximum stealth kills and minimum detection events

Off-Hand Weaving Focuses
The caster’s off-hand. Weaving Focuses are held in the non-weapon hand and amplify the player’s fate-weaving through a physical medium — crystal orbs, bound codices, carved totems, or woven thread constructs that serve as antennae for the mana well’s output. The player can equip any one-handed weapon (blade, axe, dagger, hammer) in the main hand and a Focus in the off-hand, trading shield defense for weaving amplification.

Basic amplification. Reduces mana cost of all weaves by 5%. Functional.

All branch abilities enhanced +8%. Pattern Recall echoes project from the crystal as visible light — the player can show others what they see. The first teaching tool.

Thread Reader Affinity: Pattern Recall investigations conducted through the codex require 30% less mana and reveal one additional layer of information. The codex contains Devorah’s methodology, Ezra’s framework, and the Tzur historians’ reconstruction — three scholarly traditions unified in one held object.
Complete Devorah + Ezra + Tzur quest chains
The Even Qol Cradle
Legendary

Specifically designed to hold the reassembled Even Qol. When the complete crystal is placed in the Cradle and held as a focus: all Thread Sense frequencies amplified +30%, mana well capacity +15%, and the Even Qol’s original frequency broadcasts at range — allies within 20m gain +10% weave efficiency. The crystal’s housing, made by dwarven hands for the Tikva who made the crystal whole.
Kalum + Aban Sharru engineers (post-Even Qol reassembly)
The Weaver’s Paradox
Primordial
WEAVE: +30% mana efficiency
Dual Frequency: the focus amplifies both Dav and Tav weaving simultaneously. Weaves performed through this focus carry both frequencies regardless of the player’s intent — creation weaves have structural silence, void weaves have creative warmth. The brothers’ alliance held in one hand while the other hand holds a weapon. War and peace in the same grip.
Forge: Aban Dummu Door. Requires Act VII harmonic + Harmonic Residue + Even Qol crystal dust (a fragment willingly given by the reassembled stone) + Unwriting Boundary Sample

ARMOR SLOTS — Individual Pieces
Design Note
Armor in Veilweaver occupies nine slots: Head, Shoulders, Chest (existing light/medium/heavy), Bracers, Gloves, Belt, Legs, Boots, Cloak. Each slot has individual items at every tier. Named armor sets (Section after this) draw from these pieces and add set bonuses when multiple pieces from the same set are equipped.
The existing chest armor from the Item Catalogue (Light/Medium/Heavy at six tiers each) remains unchanged. The new slots expand the player’s customization without replacing what exists.
Headpieces — Horn-Crowns and Helms
The Tikva has horns. Every headpiece in Veilweaver is designed to accommodate this — not by hiding the horns but by framing them. The visual progression from “worn leather hood that happens to have horn-holes cut in it” to “a crown of crystallized thread that makes the horns look like they were always meant to be crowned” is one of the game’s most satisfying visual arcs.

Stats
Effect
Source

Nachash Underchamber (Malkah’s personal effects, alongside her journal)

The horns themselves become the crown. A framework of aurath crystal and dwarven metalwork that amplifies the horns’ natural thread-resonance by 40%. All Thread Sense frequencies enhanced. The visual: the horns glow through the crystal framework with the player’s dominant branch color. The Tikva’s birthright, finally decorated rather than hidden.
Complete all six Echo Log entries + Even Qol reassembly + Kalum’s gift
Diadem of Both Words
Primordial
DEF: 12%
Dual Frequency: the crown channels both Dav and Tav through the horns simultaneously. Thread Sense perceives the complete dual-frequency web at all times. The horns glow gold-and-blue. The Tikva standing between two brothers, wearing both their signatures. All weaving performed while crowned costs 15% less mana.
Forge: Any site. Requires Act VII harmonic + Harmonic Residue + Fire Statue Stone Dust + Unwriting Boundary crystal

Shoulders
Shoulder pieces in Veilweaver carry the visual weight of the player’s archetype. Thread Readers wear layered observation mantles. Thread Weavers wear reinforced combat pauldrons. Thread Singers wear construction-grade supports. Thread Keepers wear broad protective spans designed to shelter the person standing next to them. The shoulder slot is where the player’s build becomes visible to NPCs at a distance.

Complete all four fallen Tikva vignettes (the gloves carry the impression of four different pairs of hands)
Gloves of the Weave-Wright
Primordial
DEF: 7%
Dual Frequency: anything the player creates while wearing these gloves carries both Dav warmth and Tav structural silence. Crafted items are one tier higher in quality. Built structures develop Living Architecture properties 50% faster. The hands that held the midpoint between two brothers, now making things that carry both.
Forge: Player-built settlement. Requires Thread Singer Mastery + all five building tiers completed + Harmonic Residue

Cloaks
The cloak slot is unique — it modifies the player’s visual profile and has the largest impact on how NPCs perceive the player at a distance. A player in a Keeper’s cloak is treated differently than a player in a Veilwarden’s cloak before any conversation begins. The cloak is the first thing the world sees.

+8% weather resistance
The cloak’s surface subtly shifts color to match the dominant thread frequency in the environment — warm gold in Dav-touched areas, cool blue in void-touched zones, faint teal near Keh’Dem influence. Environmental camouflage through thread resonance. +15% stealth in any environment.

The Cloak Between
Primordial
+15% weather resistance

When suppressed: functionally invisible to all thread-based detection. When visible: all thread-based effects amplified +20%. The cloak that holds both states and lets the player choose moment-to-moment which one they inhabit.
Manifestation: appears for players who alternated between stealth and open combat throughout the game without consistently favoring either

#### NAMED ARMOR SETS

Multi-Piece Bonuses — The Aspirational Gear
Named armor sets draw from the individual piece pool and add escalating bonuses when multiple pieces from the same set are equipped. Each set has five pieces (Head, Shoulders, Chest, Legs, Gloves) and provides bonuses at 2-piece, 3-piece, and 5-piece thresholds. The remaining slots (Bracers, Belt, Boots, Cloak) remain open for individual pieces, allowing the player to mix set bonuses with standalone equipment.
Sets are organized by tier. Higher-tier sets require proportionally more investment to acquire but provide bonuses that fundamentally change how the player interacts with the game. The Primordial sets are endgame aspirational content that rewards the most committed players with equipment that makes their archetype sing.

#### GOOD TIER SETS

Frontier Survivor’s Kit
The gear of someone who stopped waiting for Milah to help and started helping themselves.
Pieces: Frontier Cap (Head), Padded Travelling Shoulders, Frontier Brigandine (Chest), Frontier Trousers (Legs), Work Gloves (Gloves)
Bonus
Effect
2-piece
Warmth buff duration from rest and food +30%. The frontier teaches you to hold onto comfort.
3-piece
Stamina recovery +15%. Thread Sense range +10%. You learn to keep moving and keep watching.
5-piece
Frontier Grit: When health drops below 25%, damage output increases +20% and stamina costs decrease 20% for 15 seconds. Once per encounter. The frontier doesn’t let you lie down.

Source: Assembled from Yaqar-region merchants, quest rewards, and basic crafting. Available from Act I.
Dwarven Apprentice Plate
The first set of real armor the underground offers a surface-dweller. Or, in this case, a Tikva.
Pieces: Apprentice Helm (Head), Forge Shoulders, Dwarven Plate (Chest), Stoneworker’s Legs (Legs), Stone-Singer’s Grips (Gloves)
Bonus
Effect
2-piece
Thread Singer channeling speed +10%. Building in dwarven armor feels natural.
3-piece
Void Resistance +10%. DEF +5%. Dwarven alloy conducts protection the way it conducts thread.
5-piece
Foundation Stance: While standing still for 3+ seconds, DEF doubles and nearby allies gain +10% DEF. The dwarves build by standing firm. The armor teaches you the same.

Source: Aban Sharru merchants and engineers. Available from Act III.

#### SUPERIOR TIER SETS

Effect
2-piece
Void Resistance +15%. The baseline for border service.
3-piece
+15% damage to void-touched creatures. Dodge distance +10%. Fight smart, fight fast.
5-piece
Border Discipline: Killing a void-touched creature within 5 seconds of dodging its attack grants +25% attack speed for 8 seconds. The Veilwardens call this rhythm “the weave” without knowing how literally true that is.

Source: Gvul Adom garrison + Veilwarden reputation (Trusted). Available from Act IV.
Thread-Binder’s Inheritance
Handed down through families who kept the thread alive in secret. Every piece has been worn by someone who could feel the thread but had no name for what they felt.
Pieces: Thread-Binder Hood (Head), Family Mantle (Shoulders), Thread-Binder’s Coat (Chest), Ancestor’s Leggings (Legs), Thread-Binder’s Bands (Gloves — with bracers serving as gloves for this set)
Bonus
Effect
2-piece
Weave efficiency +10%. The generations before you make your weaving easier.
3-piece

5-piece
The Inherited Thread: Mana well recovery +20%. When the player performs a fate-weave, there is a 15% chance that the weave produces a secondary echo — a faint golden afterimage of the weave performed by a previous generation’s sensitive. The ancestors are weaving with you.

Source: Thread-Binder contacts + Thread-Binder reputation (Honored). Scattered acquisition across Acts II–IV.

#### EPIC TIER SETS

During Exposed, all damage to the target is doubled and their abilities are suppressed. The hunter becomes the mirror.

The Keeper’s Vestments

#### PRIMORDIAL TIER SETS

The effect is the Act VII reweaving compressed into a wearable capability. The web remembers what you did. The set lets you do it again. Smaller. But real.
